turns.content: What's the difference between a Docker image and a container? I keep conflating them.

A helpful way to separate them is: an image is a recipe (or template) and a container is a running instance made from that recipe. More concretely: - Docker image: an immutable snapshot of a filesystem + metadata (like default command, environment variables, exposed ports). Images are built from a Dockerfile and are stored in layers. You can download (pull) images, tag them, and share them. - Docker container: a runtime object created from an image. When you “run” an image, Docker creates a container, adds a thin writable layer on top of the image’s read-only layers, sets up networking, and starts the process (like your app). A quick example: - Image: ubuntu:24.04 (just the base filesystem + metadata) - Container: a specific running “ubuntu:24.04” instance, maybe you started it and installed extra packages inside it. Those changes live in that container’s writable layer unless you commit them to a new image or use volumes.
So if I “install something” inside a container, that doesn’t change the image?

Right. Installing something inside a running container changes that container’s writable layer, not the underlying image. The image layers remain unchanged. If you want those changes to become reusable, you typically do one of these: 1) Put the installation steps in a Dockerfile and rebuild the image (best practice). 2) Use docker commit to create a new image from the container’s current state (useful for quick experiments, less ideal for reproducibility).
What do you mean by layers? I’ve heard images are layered but I don’t “see” them.

Image layers are like stacked diffs of filesystem changes. Each instruction in a Dockerfile (roughly) creates a new layer containing the changes from that instruction. For example, a Dockerfile like: - FROM python:3.12-slim - RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl - COPY . /app - RUN pip install -r /app/requirements.txt This tends to produce layers: - Base image layers (from python:3.12-slim) - A layer adding curl - A layer copying your app files - A layer adding installed Python dependencies Docker stores these layers and can reuse them if nothing changed in earlier steps, which speeds up builds. You can inspect some of this via: - docker history IMAGE_NAME - docker image inspect IMAGE_NAME
If layers are read-only, how can a container write files?

Docker uses a union filesystem (like overlay2 on Linux). Think of it as: - Lower layers: the image layers (read-only) - Upper layer: a container-specific writable layer When a container writes a file: - If it’s a new file, it’s created in the writable layer. - If it modifies an existing file from a lower layer, the system does “copy-on-write”: it copies that file up into the writable layer, then modifies it there. So each container has its own writable changes without altering the shared image layers.
Does that mean two containers from the same image share the same base files?

Yes, they share the same image layers on disk. That’s one reason containers are efficient: multiple containers can start from the same image without duplicating the whole filesystem. But their writable layers are separate, so changes you make in one container won’t show up in another unless you use something like: - a named volume or bind mount to share data - a network service between them - or you rebuild/retag an image that both use
I keep hearing “containers are lightweight VMs.” Is that accurate?

It’s a common comparison, but it’s not quite accurate. - VMs virtualize hardware: each VM runs its own kernel (through a hypervisor) and has a full OS stack. - Containers virtualize at the OS level: containers share the host kernel. They isolate processes using namespaces and control resource usage with cgroups. So containers are usually: - faster to start - smaller in footprint - more “portable” at the app level (but still depend on the host kernel type; Linux containers need a Linux kernel)
Wait, if containers share the host kernel, how can I run Linux containers on macOS or Windows?

On macOS and Windows, Docker typically runs a lightweight Linux VM under the hood (via Docker Desktop). The containers still share the kernel—just the kernel of that Linux VM, not the macOS/Windows kernel. So: - On Linux: containers run directly using the host Linux kernel. - On macOS/Windows: containers run inside a Linux VM; Docker Desktop manages it.
So an image is like a file, and a container is like a process?

That’s a pretty good analogy: - Image ≈ program binary + resources (packaged filesystem + metadata) - Container ≈ running process instance (plus isolated filesystem view, network, etc.) One tweak: an image is more like a filesystem snapshot plus a default command; it’s not exactly a single binary. But “image is static / container is running” is the key mental model.
When I do docker run ubuntu, does it create a container immediately?

Yes. docker run does roughly: 1) If the image isn’t present locally, pull it. 2) Create a container from the image (docker create). 3) Start it (docker start). You can see this split: - docker create ubuntu:24.04 - docker start <container_id> And docker run combines them and optionally attaches your terminal.
What’s the difference between docker run and docker start then?

docker run creates a new container (unless you use flags that reuse something indirectly, but generally it creates a fresh one). docker start starts an existing stopped container. Typical pattern: - docker run --name mybox -it ubuntu:24.04 bash This creates container “mybox” and starts it. - exit (stops it) - docker start -ai mybox This restarts the same container with its existing writable layer intact.
If I exit a container, is it deleted?

By default, no. Exiting stops it but it remains on disk. You can list all containers (including stopped) with: - docker ps -a If you want it to be deleted when it exits, use: - docker run --rm ... That’s common for short-lived tasks.
Okay. What about docker pull and docker build? I’m confused about where images come from.

Images come from two main places: 1) Pulling from a registry: - docker pull nginx:latest This downloads an image from a registry (by default, Docker Hub unless configured otherwise). 2) Building locally from a Dockerfile: - docker build -t myapp:1.0 . This reads your Dockerfile and context (the files in the build directory), executes steps, and produces a local image. You can then run your built image with docker run myapp:1.0.
What is a registry exactly? Is Docker Hub the only one?

A registry is a service that stores and distributes images. Docker Hub is the default public registry, but there are many others: - GitHub Container Registry (ghcr.io) - Google Artifact Registry / GCR - AWS ECR - Azure Container Registry - Self-hosted Docker Registry (registry:2 image) An image name typically looks like: - [registryhost]/[namespace]/[repo]:[tag] Examples: - ubuntu:24.04 (implicit Docker Hub library) - ghcr.io/someuser/someimage:1.2.3 - localhost:5000/myimage:latest (local registry)
And tags are like versions?

Tags are labels pointing to a specific image manifest, often used like versions, but they’re not guaranteed to be immutable unless you enforce it. Examples: - python:3.12-slim (tag implies a version and variant) - latest (a moving target; can point to different image contents over time) If you want a truly pinned reference, you can use a digest: - python@sha256:... Digests uniquely identify the image content.
When I run docker images, I see REPOSITORY, TAG, IMAGE ID. Is IMAGE ID the digest?

Not exactly. - IMAGE ID shown by docker images is a local identifier (historically truncated) for the image object in your local Docker engine. - A digest (sha256:...) is a content-addressable identifier from the registry manifest. They’re related but not the same. If you pull an image, you’ll often see a digest in the pull output. You can also get repo digests via: - docker image inspect --format='{{json .RepoDigests}}' IMAGE RepoDigests typically show something like: - ["python@sha256:..."]
You mentioned Dockerfile context. What does “context” mean?

The build context is the set of files Docker can access during a build. When you run: - docker build . Docker sends the contents of the current directory (minus what’s excluded by .dockerignore) to the Docker daemon/build system. Then instructions like COPY and ADD can pull files from that context into the image. This is why it’s important to: - keep the build context small - use .dockerignore to exclude things like node_modules, .git, build artifacts, large datasets, etc.
I’ve seen ADD and COPY. What’s the difference?

Prefer COPY unless you need ADD’s extra features. - COPY: straightforward copy from build context into the image. - ADD: can also extract local tar archives automatically, and can fetch URLs (though fetching URLs in ADD is often discouraged because it’s less explicit and can hurt caching/reproducibility). So typically: - Use COPY for copying files. - Use RUN curl/wget for downloading things, because it’s explicit and easier to control.
If I build an image and then run a container, how do I update it when my code changes?

The common workflow is: 1) Edit code. 2) Rebuild the image: docker build -t myapp:dev . 3) Run a new container from the new image: docker run --rm -p 8080:8080 myapp:dev For faster iteration during development, people often use bind mounts so your code is on the host and the container reads it live: - docker run --rm -it -v "$PWD":/app -w /app python:3.12 python app.py That way you don’t rebuild for every code change, though you might still rebuild when dependencies change.
Bind mounts and volumes confuse me too. Are those part of images or containers?

They’re attached to containers, not baked into images (though images can declare mount points with VOLUME, which influences behavior). Two main types: 1) Bind mount: maps a host path to a container path. - -v /host/path:/container/path or - --mount type=bind,src=/host/path,dst=/container/path Good for: local development, sharing specific files/folders. 2) Named volume: managed by Docker, stored in Docker’s volume directory. - -v mydata:/var/lib/postgresql/data or - --mount type=volume,src=mydata,dst=/var/lib/postgresql/data Good for: persistent data that shouldn’t depend on a specific host path; easier portability.
If I delete a container, do I lose the volume data?

Depends on the type and how you delete. - If you used a named volume (e.g., mydata:/data), deleting the container does NOT delete the volume by default. The data remains until you remove the volume explicitly (docker volume rm mydata) or prune unused volumes (docker volume prune). - If you used a bind mount, the data is just on the host filesystem; deleting the container doesn’t remove host files. - If you used an anonymous volume (Docker creates a random volume name), it also persists unless you used --rm or remove it explicitly. docker rm -v CONTAINER removes volumes attached to that container.
I ran docker run -p 8080:80 nginx and it worked. What is -p doing exactly?

-p publishes a container port to the host. - -p 8080:80 means: map host port 8080 to container port 80. So when you visit http://localhost:8080 on your host, Docker forwards traffic to port 80 inside the container. A few notes: - Container port 80 must have a process listening inside the container (nginx listens on 80). - You can also specify IP: -p 127.0.0.1:8080:80 (bind only on localhost). - Without -p, the container might still be reachable from other containers on the same Docker network, but not from the host via that port mapping.
Is EXPOSE in Dockerfile the same as -p?

Not the same. - EXPOSE 80 is documentation/metadata: it indicates the container intends to listen on port 80. - -p actually publishes the port to the host. There’s also: - docker run -P (capital P) which publishes all exposed ports to random host ports. So EXPOSE can be helpful, but it doesn’t open ports by itself.
Networks: I see “bridge” network. Do containers always use that?

By default, yes: if you don’t specify a network, containers go on the default bridge network. Docker networks: - bridge: default for standalone containers; NATed. - host: container shares host networking (Linux only; removes isolation for networking). - none: no networking. - user-defined bridge: you create your own bridge network; containers on it get automatic DNS-based service discovery by container name. For multi-container setups, user-defined bridge networks are usually better than the default bridge because: - built-in DNS works reliably - isolation is clearer - you can control subnets and settings
DNS by container name? How does that work?

On a user-defined bridge network, Docker runs an embedded DNS server. Containers can resolve each other by container name (or network alias). Example: - docker network create mynet - docker run -d --name db --network mynet postgres:16 - docker run -it --rm --name app --network mynet alpine:3.20 sh Inside app, you can do: - ping db and it should resolve to the db container’s IP on that network. On the default bridge network, name resolution is more limited and historically required linking; it’s better to use a user-defined network.
Is Docker Compose required for multiple containers?

Not required, but it makes multi-container setups easier. Without Compose, you can do everything with docker run commands and docker network create, but it gets verbose. Compose lets you define services, networks, volumes in a YAML file and run: - docker compose up - docker compose down It’s especially helpful for: - setting environment variables - creating a network automatically - defining dependencies - persistent volumes - restart policies
I tried Compose once and got confused between “service” and “container.”

In Compose: - A service is a definition/template: image/build, ports, environment, volumes, etc. - A container is a runtime instance of a service. By default, docker compose up creates one container per service. But you can scale: - docker compose up --scale web=3 Then you’ll have multiple containers for the “web” service. The service name is like a logical group.
If a service has 3 containers, how do ports work? You can’t bind 3 containers to the same host port.

Exactly. If you publish a fixed host port like "8080:80", only one container can bind host port 8080. Common approaches: 1) Don’t publish each replica directly; put a reverse proxy/load balancer in front (another container) that publishes the host port and routes internally. 2) Publish to random host ports (Compose can do "0:80" in some contexts, or omit host port and let Docker assign). 3) Use an orchestrator like Kubernetes/Swarm which provides service abstractions for load balancing. In basic Compose, the typical pattern is one published port per service, so scaling a service that publishes a fixed port needs extra routing.
Okay, back to images vs containers. Are containers “ephemeral”? People say that a lot.

“Ephemeral” in container talk usually means: you should be able to destroy and recreate containers at any time without losing important state. In practice: - The container filesystem (writable layer) is treated as disposable. - Persistent state should be in volumes or external services. You can still keep a container around and it will keep its writable changes, but that’s not the recommended way to store important data long-term.
Then why would I ever exec into a container and edit files?

It’s fine for debugging or quick experiments. But if you rely on manual edits inside a container, you lose reproducibility. Next time you rebuild or redeploy, those changes won’t be there. Better patterns: - Put changes in the Dockerfile. - Use bind mounts for editable code. - Use environment variables for configuration. - Use volumes for data. Exec is still useful for: - inspecting logs/config - running one-off commands - checking filesystem layout - connecting to a shell for troubleshooting
What’s the difference between docker exec and docker attach?

- docker exec runs a new command in an already running container (like starting a new process). Example: docker exec -it mycontainer sh - docker attach attaches your terminal to the container’s main process’s STDIN/STDOUT. If you attach and then exit, you might stop the main process depending on how you exit and how it handles signals. Generally: - Use exec for shells/debugging. - Use attach if you specifically want to interact with the main process.
If the container’s “main process” exits, the container stops, right?

Yes. A container’s lifecycle is tied to its PID 1 process (the command you run). When that process exits, Docker considers the container stopped. That’s why images often specify a long-running command (like a server). If you run something like: - docker run ubuntu:24.04 echo hi It prints “hi” and exits immediately, so the container stops.
PID 1 has special behavior, I heard. Is that true?

Yes, in Linux PID 1 has special signal-handling and zombie-reaping responsibilities. Common container pitfalls: - If your app is PID 1 and doesn’t handle SIGTERM properly, docker stop may not shut it down gracefully. - If PID 1 doesn’t reap child processes, you can accumulate zombie processes. Solutions: - Use a proper init system in the container (like tini) or ensure your app handles signals and reaps children. - Many official images already use tini or have good defaults; or you can run with: docker run --init ... which uses Docker’s built-in tini.
That’s interesting. So what actually is inside an image besides files?

An image includes: - Filesystem layers (the root filesystem content) - Metadata/config, such as: - Entrypoint (ENTRYPOINT) - Default command (CMD) - Environment variables (ENV) - Working directory (WORKDIR) - User (USER) - Exposed ports (EXPOSE) - Labels (LABEL) - Volumes (VOLUME) - Stop signal (STOPSIGNAL) When you create a container, Docker combines the image config with container-specific settings you supply at run time: - port mappings - mounts - environment overrides - resource limits - container name - network settings
ENTRYPOINT vs CMD is another one I mix up. Can you explain it?

Think of ENTRYPOINT as “the executable” and CMD as “default arguments” (or default command) that can be overridden. Two forms: exec form (recommended) and shell form. Common pattern: - ENTRYPOINT ["python", "-m", "myapp"] - CMD ["--help"] Then: - docker run image           runs: python -m myapp --help - docker run image --version runs: python -m myapp --version (CMD replaced/extended depending on form) If you only set CMD and no ENTRYPOINT: - CMD can be overridden completely by specifying a command in docker run. You can override entrypoint at runtime too: - docker run --entrypoint sh image
Why does shell form matter?

Shell form runs through a shell (e.g., /bin/sh -c), which changes signal handling and argument parsing. Example: - CMD echo hello This becomes /bin/sh -c "echo hello" Exec form: - CMD ["echo", "hello"] Exec form: - avoids shell quirks - handles signals more predictably (important for graceful shutdown) - avoids issues with spaces/quoting Shell form can be convenient for simple commands but is less robust.
If I want to run a command in a container without starting the default app, what do I do?

You can override CMD by specifying a command at the end of docker run, or override ENTRYPOINT. Examples: - Override CMD: docker run --rm myimage ls -la This replaces the default command (CMD) but keeps ENTRYPOINT if set (depending on how it’s defined). - Override ENTRYPOINT: docker run --rm --entrypoint sh myimage -c "ls -la" If you’re not sure what the image uses, inspect: - docker image inspect myimage Look for Config.Entrypoint and Config.Cmd.
How does caching work in docker build? Sometimes it rebuilds everything.

Docker build cache works per layer. If a layer’s inputs change, that layer and all subsequent layers are rebuilt. Inputs include: - the Dockerfile instruction itself - files referenced by COPY/ADD (their content/metadata) - build arguments (ARG) used in that step - base image digest (if it changed and you pulled a newer one) Common ways to improve caching: - Put infrequently changing steps earlier (install OS packages, dependencies). - Copy dependency manifests first (e.g., package.json, requirements.txt), install deps, then copy the rest of the source. - Use .dockerignore to avoid sending irrelevant files that might change often.
Can you give a concrete example with Node?

Sure, typical pattern: Bad for caching: - COPY . . - RUN npm install Any code change invalidates COPY ., forcing npm install again. Better: - COPY package*.json ./ - RUN npm ci - COPY . . Now npm ci only reruns when package.json/package-lock.json changes, not when you edit application source files.
I’m on Windows. Does that change anything about volumes and line endings?

It can. Bind mounts on Windows can have: - path syntax differences (C:\path vs /c/path depending on shell) - performance differences (bind mounts into Linux VM can be slower) - line endings (CRLF) may cause issues with shell scripts inside Linux containers (e.g., /bin/sh^M errors) Common fixes: - Configure Git to use LF for scripts (or use .gitattributes). - Ensure scripts are executable and have correct shebang. - Use WSL2-based Docker Desktop for better filesystem integration. - For paths, use the syntax your terminal expects (PowerShell vs Git Bash vs WSL).
I also heard about “multi-stage builds.” What problem do they solve?

Multi-stage builds let you use one stage to build/compile something with heavy toolchains, and a later stage to package only the final artifacts into a smaller runtime image. Example idea: - Stage 1: use a full build image (gcc, node, go toolchain) to compile. - Stage 2: use a slim runtime base and copy the compiled binary. Benefits: - smaller final image - fewer dependencies and attack surface - clearer separation of build vs runtime
Could you sketch a simple multi-stage Dockerfile?

Example for a Go app: FROM golang:1.22 AS build WORKDIR /src COPY go.mod go.sum ./ RUN go mod download COPY . . RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -o /out/app ./cmd/app FROM alpine:3.20 COPY --from=build /out/app /usr/local/bin/app ENTRYPOINT ["/usr/local/bin/app"] Final image contains just the binary and Alpine base, not the Go toolchain.
If images are immutable, how do updates happen? Like security patches?

You rebuild images with updated base layers and dependencies. Typical flow: - Base images (like ubuntu, alpine, python) publish updates. - You rebuild your image so it pulls in updated base layers (or at least uses a newer digest). - You redeploy containers from the new image. That’s why it’s important to: - rebuild regularly - pin versions thoughtfully (not too loose, not too rigid) - scan images for vulnerabilities if you care about security
What does “pin versions thoughtfully” mean?

If you use tags like latest, your builds can change unexpectedly. If you pin everything to exact versions/digests, you get reproducibility but must update intentionally. A balanced approach: - Pin major/minor versions (python:3.12-slim instead of latest). - Use lockfiles (package-lock.json, poetry.lock, etc.). - For highly controlled environments, pin base image digests too, then update them intentionally.
Are containers actually secure? Like, can they escape?

Containers provide isolation, but it’s not the same as a VM boundary. Security depends on: - kernel vulnerabilities (containers share kernel) - container configuration (privileged mode, capabilities, mounts) - runtime hardening (seccomp, AppArmor/SELinux, user namespaces) - image contents (vulnerable packages) - running as root vs non-root You can improve security by: - avoiding --privileged - dropping capabilities (e.g., --cap-drop=ALL, then add what you need) - running as a non-root user (USER in Dockerfile) - using read-only filesystem where possible (--read-only) - using minimal base images - scanning images and updating
What is --privileged anyway?

--privileged gives the container almost all capabilities and lifts many restrictions, making it much closer to having full access to the host. It’s sometimes used for low-level tasks (like running Docker-in-Docker or accessing hardware), but it greatly increases risk. If you need a specific capability, it’s better to add only that: - --cap-add=NET_ADMIN rather than privileged.
I saw “capabilities” mentioned. Are those like permissions?

Yes, Linux capabilities split the all-powerful root privileges into smaller units (CAP_NET_ADMIN, CAP_SYS_ADMIN, etc.). Containers typically run with a limited set of capabilities by default. You can view/adjust them with: - --cap-add - --cap-drop CAP_SYS_ADMIN is especially powerful and often considered “almost root.”
Switching gears: what happens when I run docker system prune? I’m scared of it.

docker system prune removes unused Docker objects. By default it removes: - stopped containers - unused networks - dangling images (images not tagged and not referenced) - build cache It does NOT remove: - named volumes by default If you add: - docker system prune --volumes then it can remove unused volumes too, which can delete data you care about if volumes aren’t currently attached. Safer commands: - docker container prune (stopped containers) - docker image prune (dangling images) - docker volume ls and docker volume inspect to check before removing
What is a dangling image?

A dangling image is an untagged image layer set that isn’t referenced by any tag. Often created when you rebuild an image with the same tag; the old image loses its tag and becomes dangling. You can list dangling images: - docker images -f dangling=true They can usually be removed safely unless referenced by containers (Docker won’t remove images used by containers).
Sometimes I can’t delete an image because it says it’s in use. Even if the container is stopped.

Stopped containers still reference the image they were created from. So Docker prevents deleting that image until you remove the containers using it. Fix: - docker ps -a (find containers) - docker rm <container> Then: - docker rmi <image> Or remove all stopped containers: - docker container prune
If I remove an image, does it remove the layers too?
Docker removes layers that are no longer referenced by any other images. Since layers can be shared, removing one image might not free all the disk space if other images still use those layers.
Is there a way to see disk usage by Docker?
Yes: - docker system df It shows space used by: - images - containers - local volumes - build cache You can add -v for more detail: - docker system df -v
I tried building an image and got “COPY failed: file not found in build context.” But the file exists.

Common causes: - You’re running docker build from a different directory than you think, so the build context doesn’t include that file. - .dockerignore excludes it. - Path case mismatch (especially on Linux). - You used an absolute path in COPY; COPY only works with paths relative to the build context. Checks: - Verify you’re in the directory you think: pwd - Check .dockerignore - Ensure Dockerfile path and build context align: docker build -f path/to/Dockerfile path/to/context
Why can’t COPY use absolute paths? That seems annoying.

It’s a security and reproducibility thing. If Docker could copy arbitrary host paths, builds could accidentally depend on files outside the project directory, and it would be harder to reproduce elsewhere. Build context acts as a boundary: only files you intentionally send are available to the build.
What about secrets? If context gets sent, does that mean my .env might be baked in?

It can be if you COPY it, or if you accidentally include it in the context and then do something with it. Even if you don’t copy it, sending secrets in the build context is risky, especially if you use remote builders. Best practices: - Put secrets in .dockerignore (e.g., .env, id_rsa). - Don’t bake secrets into images with ENV in Dockerfile. - Use runtime environment variables (docker run -e ...) or Compose env_file for local use. - For build-time secrets, use BuildKit secrets: docker build --secret id=mysecret,src=./secretfile . and in Dockerfile: RUN --mount=type=secret,id=mysecret cat /run/secrets/mysecret ...
BuildKit? Is that different from normal build?

BuildKit is the newer build backend for Docker that provides better performance and features like: - parallel build steps - improved caching - secrets and SSH mounts - more flexible output options It’s often enabled by default in modern Docker versions. You can tell by the build output format. You can explicitly enable with: - DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build ...
I saw “--mount=type=cache” in a Dockerfile example. What does that do?

That’s a BuildKit feature to persist cache directories between builds without baking them into the image. Example: RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/pip pip install -r requirements.txt This lets pip reuse downloaded wheels across builds, speeding things up, while keeping the final image cleaner. It’s especially useful for package managers (pip, npm, apt caches, etc.), though you need to use it carefully to avoid stale artifacts.
How does apt-get caching work in Docker? People say to combine apt-get update and install.

apt-get update downloads package index files. If you do: RUN apt-get update RUN apt-get install -y curl Those are separate layers. If the second layer is cached, it might reuse an old apt-get update layer even though indexes are stale, causing “package not found” or outdated packages. Standard pattern: RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends curl \ && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* This ensures update/install happen together in one layer and cleans up apt lists to reduce image size.
Does rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* actually reduce size? It’s still in the layer history, right?

It reduces size because it removes the files before the layer is committed. In a single RUN instruction, the layer contains the final filesystem state after all commands. So if you download lists and then delete them within the same RUN, they won’t persist in that layer. But if you had them in an earlier layer, deleting in a later layer wouldn’t reduce the size of the earlier layer—it would just hide them in the merged filesystem view. That’s why combining commands matters.
That makes sense. Is there a similar thing for npm?

Yes: - Use npm ci instead of npm install when you have a lockfile; it’s more reproducible and often faster. - Clean npm cache if needed (though npm cache is not always huge). - Multi-stage builds can keep dev dependencies out of the final image. Example approach: - In build stage: install deps, build assets. - In runtime stage: copy only built assets and production deps.
I’m also confused about “scratch” images. What are those?

scratch is an empty base image. It’s used when you want a minimal final image, typically for statically compiled binaries (Go, Rust with static linking, etc.). Example: FROM scratch COPY app /app ENTRYPOINT ["/app"] No shell, no package manager, no CA certificates by default—so you need to copy in everything you need (like certs) or choose a minimal base like distroless/alpine.
Distroless?

Distroless images (from Google) contain only the runtime dependencies, not a full OS userland. They typically lack: - package managers - shells - many common debugging tools They’re good for reducing attack surface and size. The tradeoff is debugging is harder; you often debug with a separate debug image or by temporarily using a fuller base during development.
If there’s no shell, how do you exec into it?

You often can’t exec a shell because there isn’t one. Options: - Use docker exec to run the app’s own debug endpoints/logging. - Attach a sidecar/debug container on the same network (in orchestrators). - Rebuild the image with a shell for debugging (temporarily). - Use “distroless debug” variants if available. For local learning, it’s fine to stick to slim Debian/Alpine first.
How do environment variables work in Docker? If I set ENV in Dockerfile and also pass -e, which wins?

Runtime environment variables passed with -e override ENV values from the image. Order of precedence (simplified): - docker run -e KEY=VALUE (highest) - environment in Compose service definition - ENV in Dockerfile (default) Also, some images have entrypoint scripts that read env vars and generate config.
Is there a way to see environment variables inside a running container?
Yes: - docker exec -it container env or - docker exec -it container printenv or - docker inspect container (shows configured env, but not necessarily what processes set at runtime)
How do I see what command a container is running?

Use: - docker ps (shows the command truncated) - docker inspect --format='{{.Config.Entrypoint}} {{.Config.Cmd}}' container - docker inspect container (look at Config and Path/Args) Also inside: - docker exec container ps aux (if ps exists)
Sometimes my container exits immediately and I don’t know why. How do I debug that?

Common steps: 1) Check logs: docker logs <container> 2) Inspect exit code: docker inspect <container> --format='{{.State.ExitCode}}' 3) Run interactively to see errors: docker run -it --rm <image> sh Then run the command manually. 4) Override entrypoint to get a shell: docker run -it --rm --entrypoint sh <image> 5) Ensure the command exists and is executable, and paths are correct. Also check if it’s failing due to missing config/env vars.
If I do docker logs, does it show all logs?

It shows what the container’s main process wrote to stdout/stderr. That’s the standard logging approach in containers. If your app writes logs to files inside the container, docker logs won’t show those unless something tails them to stdout. Best practice is to log to stdout/stderr, and let the container runtime handle log collection.
What about log drivers? I saw something about json-file.

Docker supports log drivers; default is often json-file, which stores logs on disk in JSON format. Other drivers: - local (more efficient local storage) - syslog - journald - fluentd - gelf - awslogs, etc. You can configure per-container: - docker run --log-driver local ... or globally in daemon settings.
Does logging affect performance?

It can, especially if: - logs are very verbose - log files grow large - you use synchronous drivers that block Good practices: - limit log size/rotation (json-file has options like max-size, max-file) - avoid excessive debug logging in production-like scenarios - use an efficient log driver if needed
You keep saying “best practice,” but I’m just learning. What’s the simplest mental model to keep images and containers straight?

A simple set of rules: - Image = blueprint. You build it (docker build) or download it (docker pull). It doesn’t change when you run it. - Container = a running (or stopped) instance created from an image. It has a writable layer and runtime settings. - If you want changes to persist across container recreation, use: - Dockerfile rebuild for app changes - volumes for data - Containers can be deleted and recreated; images are the reusable artifact. If you remember “image is what you ship; container is what you run,” that helps.
If I stop a container and start it again, is it the same container?

Yes, if you use docker start on the same container, it’s the same container: same writable layer, same container ID, same volume attachments, though IP addresses can change depending on network. If you instead run docker run again, you get a new container with a new writable layer and usually a new ID.
Are container IDs random?
They’re generated identifiers (hash-like). Docker often shows a shortened prefix. You can reference containers by: - full ID - shortened ID (if unambiguous) - name (best for humans)
When you say IP can change, does that mean I shouldn’t rely on container IPs?

Correct. Container IPs are typically dynamic. Use: - container names via Docker DNS (on user-defined networks) - published ports to reach from host - or service discovery mechanisms (in orchestrators) Avoid hardcoding container IPs.
What happens if two containers need to talk to each other and I’m not using Compose?

You can still do it manually: 1) Create a network: docker network create mynet 2) Run containers on that network: docker run -d --name db --network mynet postgres:16 docker run -d --name app --network mynet myapp:dev 3) In app, connect to host “db” on the appropriate port. That’s basically what Compose automates.
For databases, you said persistent data should be in volumes. How do I set that up?

Example with PostgreSQL: docker volume create pgdata docker run -d --name pg \ -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=secret \ -v pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data \ -p 5432:5432 \ postgres:16 Now the database files are stored in the named volume pgdata. If you remove and recreate the container with the same volume, the data persists.
If I rebuild my app image, does that affect running containers?

Not automatically. Running containers keep using the image they were created from (by image ID). If you rebuild and retag with the same tag (e.g., myapp:dev), the tag will point to the new image, but existing containers keep running with the old one. To use the new image: - stop/remove the old container - run a new container from the updated image
That’s subtle. So tags are like pointers that can move.
Exactly. Tags are mutable references. The container references an image by its immutable ID/digest at creation time.
Can I have two images with the same tag?

Locally, a tag points to only one image at a time. When you build a new image with the same tag, Docker retargets that tag to the new image. The old image may remain without a tag (dangling) if nothing else references it.
How do I “name” an image properly? Is myapp:1.0 enough?

For local use, yes. Naming format: - repository:tag Examples: - myapp:1.0 - myapp:dev If you plan to push to a registry, you’ll include a namespace and registry: - docker.io/yourname/myapp:1.0 (Docker Hub) - ghcr.io/yourname/myapp:1.0 Then: - docker push ghcr.io/yourname/myapp:1.0
What does docker tag do then?
docker tag creates another tag (another name) pointing to the same image. Example: - docker tag myapp:dev myapp:1.0 Now both tags refer to the same image ID (until one is retagged by another build).
If containers are instances, can I “clone” a container?

Not exactly in a first-class way, but you can approximate: - Create a new image from it with docker commit, then run new containers from that image. - Or inspect its configuration and run a new container with similar settings. - If you want the filesystem state, commit captures it (minus mounted volumes). But again, Dockerfile-based rebuilds are preferred.
You keep saying commit is not ideal. Why?

Because it’s not reproducible and doesn’t document the steps. A Dockerfile acts like a build script you can review, version, and rebuild anywhere. docker commit captures a result, but not the process. Commit can also include accidental state (temporary files, caches) and you might not remember what you changed.
What about exporting a container? I saw docker export and docker save.

They’re different: - docker save exports an image (including layers and metadata) to a tar file. You can load it with docker load. Good for moving images between machines. docker save -o myapp.tar myapp:1.0 docker load -i myapp.tar - docker export exports a container’s filesystem as a tar, but it flattens it (no layer history, less metadata). You import it as a new image with docker import, but you lose image config like CMD/ENTRYPOINT unless you specify. docker export container > fs.tar cat fs.tar | docker import - myimage:imported Typically, save/load is preferred for images.
How does docker run know what to execute? Is it CMD?

Docker uses the image config: - ENTRYPOINT + CMD combine to form the final command. If ENTRYPOINT is set, it’s the base executable; CMD provides default args. If ENTRYPOINT is not set, CMD is the command. At runtime, docker run can override CMD by specifying a command. ENTRYPOINT can be overridden with --entrypoint.
What about WORKDIR? Does it create the directory?

WORKDIR sets the working directory for subsequent instructions in the Dockerfile and for the container’s default working dir at runtime. If the directory doesn’t exist, Docker creates it (within the image filesystem) when building. Example: WORKDIR /app Then COPY . . copies into /app, and CMD runs from /app unless overridden.
If I do RUN cd /app in Dockerfile, does that persist?

No, because each RUN instruction runs in its own shell/session (and creates a new layer). So: RUN cd /app RUN pwd won’t show /app; it will show the default directory again. Use WORKDIR instead, or chain commands in one RUN: RUN cd /app && do-something
That explains some weirdness I had. Is there a similar gotcha with environment variables?

ENV persists in the image for subsequent layers and runtime. But variables set only in a RUN shell session without ENV won’t persist. Example: RUN export FOO=bar RUN echo $FOO This won’t work as expected. Instead: ENV FOO=bar RUN echo $FOO Or in one RUN: RUN export FOO=bar && echo $FOO
I’m trying to understand how Docker isolates processes. What are namespaces?

Linux namespaces provide isolation by giving a process its own view of certain system resources. Containers typically use namespaces for: - pid: process IDs (process tree isolation) - net: network interfaces, IP addresses - mnt: mount points/filesystem - uts: hostname/domainname - ipc: interprocess communication - user: user/group IDs mapping (optional; improves security) So a process in a container thinks it’s in its own environment, but it’s sharing the same kernel.
And cgroups are for resource limits?

Exactly. cgroups (control groups) limit and account for resource usage: - CPU shares/quotas - memory limits - block I/O - pids limit Docker flags: - --memory 512m - --cpus 1.5 - --pids-limit 100 Resource limits are important to prevent one container from consuming all host resources.
If I set a memory limit, what happens when the container exceeds it?

Typically the kernel’s OOM (out-of-memory) killer will terminate processes in that cgroup; often your container will be killed and exit with a specific code (commonly 137). You’ll see it in docker inspect under State.OOMKilled.
Does Docker run on ARM? Like on a Raspberry Pi?

Yes. Many images have multi-architecture support. When you pull an image like nginx, the registry can serve the correct architecture variant (amd64, arm64, etc.) via a manifest list. You can check: - docker buildx imagetools inspect imagename And when building: - docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 ...
buildx is new to me. Is it required?

Not required for basic builds. buildx is Docker’s extended build tool (BuildKit-based) that supports: - multi-platform builds - advanced caching - remote builders For learning basics, docker build is enough. When you want multi-arch images, buildx becomes useful.
If I’m on an M1/M2 Mac, do I get arm64 images by default?

Usually yes. Docker will pull arm64 variants if available. If an image doesn’t have arm64 support, Docker can sometimes run amd64 images via emulation (qemu), but it may be slower and occasionally incompatible. You can force platform: - docker run --platform linux/amd64 ...
Can that cause weird bugs?
It can. Emulation might expose performance issues, and some binaries might behave differently. For reliable behavior, prefer native architecture images when possible.
Back to the writable layer: if I write a lot of data to the container filesystem, is that bad?

It can be: - The writable layer uses a storage driver (like overlay2). Heavy write workloads can be slower than writing to a volume. - Data in the writable layer is tied to the container lifecycle; if you remove the container, data is gone. - It can grow your Docker disk usage unexpectedly. For databases or heavy writes, use volumes.
If I use a volume, is it faster?

Often yes, especially for heavy I/O, because volumes are managed more directly by the filesystem and avoid some overlay copy-on-write overhead. Performance varies by OS and Docker setup (especially on macOS/Windows where there’s a VM boundary).
How do I see where volumes are stored?

On Linux, Docker typically stores volumes under: - /var/lib/docker/volumes/ You can inspect a volume: - docker volume inspect mydata It shows the Mountpoint path (on the Docker host). On macOS/Windows with Docker Desktop, that path is inside the Linux VM, not directly on your host filesystem.
I once tried to edit volume files directly and couldn’t find them on my Mac. That explains it.

Exactly—Docker Desktop hides the VM filesystem. For editing data, it’s usually better to: - use bind mounts if you need host access - or use docker exec and tools inside containers to inspect - or use docker cp to copy files in/out
docker cp? Like copying files from container to host?
Yes: - docker cp container:/path/in/container ./localpath - docker cp ./localfile container:/path/in/container Useful for quick extraction of logs/artifacts or injecting files for debugging.
Are images always Linux? What about Windows containers?

There are Windows container images too, but they require Windows container mode and a compatible Windows host. Windows containers use Windows kernel features and have different base images (like nanoserver, servercore). On Docker Desktop for Windows, you can switch between Linux and Windows container mode, but you generally can’t run both types simultaneously in the same Docker engine context.
So if I stick with Linux containers, I’m good for most things?
Yes, Linux containers are the most common and have the broadest ecosystem.

I want to test my understanding with a scenario. Say I run docker run -it python:3.12-slim and then pip install requests. That’s only in that container. If I delete it and run again, requests is gone. Right?

Correct. Unless you: - commit the container to a new image (not recommended as primary method), or - build a new image with a Dockerfile that installs requests, or - use a volume/bind mount to persist the Python environment (possible but usually not ideal for Python packages unless you’re intentionally caching wheels/venv) Typical is Dockerfile: FROM python:3.12-slim RUN pip install --no-cache-dir requests CMD ["python"] Then build and run.
If I do pip install --user, does that change anything?

It changes where the package is installed inside the container (e.g., /root/.local), but it’s still within the container’s writable layer unless that path is mounted as a volume. So it still disappears when the container is removed.
I think I get it now. But what about “base images” and “parent images”? Are they just images too?

Yes. A base image is just an image you start FROM in a Dockerfile. “Parent image” refers to the image your image builds upon. Example: FROM ubuntu:24.04 Your image’s layers are added on top of ubuntu’s layers. Ubuntu is the base/parent.
If ubuntu updates, do I automatically get updates?

Not automatically in an existing local image. You get updates when you pull a newer ubuntu:24.04 (if the tag moved) and rebuild your image. If you never pull or rebuild, your local image stays the same.
But earlier you said tags can move. So ubuntu:24.04 might change over time?
It can. Maintainers may push updated images under the same tag to include security fixes. That’s why reproducible builds sometimes pin by digest.
Is it bad that tags move?

Not inherently. Moving tags allow security updates without changing tags. The tradeoff is reproducibility: a build today might produce a different image than a build next month even with the same Dockerfile.
If I want reproducibility, should I always use digests?

It depends on your goals. For learning and many personal projects, tags are fine. For strict reproducibility, pin digests and update intentionally. A middle ground is: - Use versioned tags (not latest) - Rebuild periodically and test - Optionally record digests in a lockfile-like mechanism
I’ve seen .dockerignore but I’m not sure what to put in it.

Common entries: - .git - node_modules - __pycache__ - *.pyc - dist - build - .env - *.log - .DS_Store - coverage - .pytest_cache Basically: anything large, generated, or secret that shouldn’t be sent to the build context.
If I exclude node_modules, how does my app get dependencies?

You typically install dependencies during the image build (RUN npm ci). You copy in package.json/package-lock.json, run npm ci, then copy your source. So you don’t need host node_modules inside the image. In fact, copying host node_modules can cause platform mismatches.
Platform mismatches?

Node modules can include native binaries compiled for a specific OS/architecture. If you copy node_modules from your host (say macOS) into a Linux container, native modules might not work. Building/installing inside the container ensures they match the container’s environment.
That’s a good point. I think I tried that once and it broke.
Very common first-time issue.
What about Python? Similar issues with compiled wheels?
Yes. Some Python packages have compiled components. Installing inside the container ensures you get Linux-compatible wheels or compile against the container’s libraries.
I want to understand container startup a bit more. When I run docker run, what happens first: filesystem, network, then process?

Roughly: 1) Docker creates the container root filesystem from image layers + writable layer. 2) Sets up namespaces/cgroups. 3) Configures mounts (volumes/binds). 4) Configures networking (connects to network, assigns IP). 5) Sets environment variables, working dir, user, etc. 6) Starts the container’s main process.
If I mount a volume over a path that already has files in the image, what happens?

The mount hides what’s underneath. Example: If the image has files in /app, and you mount a volume to /app, you’ll see the volume contents instead of the image’s /app content. This can surprise people: - You build image with code in /app - Then run with -v "$PWD":/app Now the container sees your host directory at /app, not the built-in code.
Is there a way to “merge” them?

Not directly. Mounts replace the view at that path. Workarounds: - Mount to a different path - Copy files from image to volume on first run (entrypoint script pattern) - Use bind mount only for specific subdirectories/files
I’ve heard of “init containers” in Kubernetes, but that’s probably beyond this.
It’s beyond basic Docker, but the idea is similar: run a step to prepare volumes before the main app starts.
If I want to keep things simple: one image, one container. What commands should I practice?

A solid starter set: - docker pull IMAGE - docker images - docker run (with -it, --rm, -p, -v) - docker ps / docker ps -a - docker stop / docker start - docker logs - docker exec - docker build - docker rm / docker rmi - docker system df Practicing those will make images vs containers feel natural.
Can you give me a mini exercise to test images vs containers understanding?

Try this: 1) Run an interactive container and create a file: docker run -it --name testbox ubuntu:24.04 bash echo "hello" > /tmp/hi.txt exit 2) Start it again and check the file: docker start -ai testbox cat /tmp/hi.txt exit You should still see it, because it’s the same container. 3) Run a new container from the same image and check: docker run --rm -it ubuntu:24.04 bash cat /tmp/hi.txt It should not exist, because it’s a different container. 4) Remove the original container: docker rm testbox Now the file is gone with it. This shows: container writable layer persists across stops/starts, but not across deletion or new containers.
That’s helpful. If I want the file to persist even if I remove the container, I’d use a volume?

Exactly. Repeat the exercise but mount a named volume: docker volume create mytmp docker run -it --name testbox2 -v mytmp:/data ubuntu:24.04 bash echo "hello" > /data/hi.txt exit docker rm testbox2 docker run --rm -it -v mytmp:/data ubuntu:24.04 bash cat /data/hi.txt The file should still be there.
Nice. One more confusion: what’s the difference between “docker build” and “docker compose build”?

docker build builds one image from a Dockerfile and context you specify. docker compose build builds images for services defined in a compose.yaml that have a build: section. It can build multiple images, pass build args, and use the service definitions. Compose also handles: - naming images according to project/service - building with dependencies if configured If your compose file references images from a registry without build:, compose build won’t do anything for those.
Sometimes Compose names images weirdly. Like project_service.

Compose uses a project name (often directory name) and service name to name resources: - containers: project-service-1 - networks: project_default - volumes: project_volume You can override project name: - docker compose -p myproj up And you can set explicit image name in compose.yaml: services: web: image: myweb:dev build: .
Is it possible to run a container without an image?
Not really. A container is created from an image. Even if you import a filesystem tar, it becomes an image first.
So image is always the starting point.
Yes—image is the immutable base; container is the runtime instance derived from it.
I think I finally have the mental split. If I forget, I’ll remind myself: image is like a class, container is like an object instance.

That’s another good analogy: - Class (image) defines what it looks like. - Object (container) is an instantiated thing with its own state (writable layer, runtime config). Just remember that “state” in the container should ideally be treated as ephemeral unless stored in volumes.
Last question: why are images so big sometimes? If it’s just layers, can’t it be tiny?

Image size depends on: - base image choice (alpine vs ubuntu vs full language runtimes) - installed packages and build tools - leftover caches (apt lists, package manager caches) - copying unnecessary files (large context, artifacts) - not using multi-stage builds (shipping compilers/toolchains) To reduce size: - pick slim base images - clean caches in the same RUN step - use multi-stage builds - use .dockerignore - avoid copying test data, docs, etc. into runtime images
Great. I’m going to try those exercises. If I get stuck on a specific command output, I’ll ask.
Sounds good—if you paste the commands you ran and the output, I can help interpret what’s happening in terms of images, containers, layers, and mounts.
When should I use git rebase instead of merge? I keep hearing conflicting advice.

Rebase and merge both combine changes from one line of development into another, but they do it differently. - merge creates a new “merge commit” that has two parents. It preserves the fact that two histories diverged and then came back together. - rebase “replays” commits from one branch on top of another base commit, rewriting commit IDs in the process. It makes history look linear, as if the work was done on top of the latest base all along. A practical rule of thumb: - Use merge when you want to preserve the exact historical branching structure or when you’re integrating completed work and don’t want to rewrite history. - Use rebase when you want to keep a clean linear history for a series of local commits before sharing them, or when you want to update a feature branch with the latest base changes without introducing a merge commit. If you tell me what your usual workflow looks like (e.g., you have a main branch and short-lived topic branches, or you do long-lived branches), I can suggest a consistent approach.
I mostly have a main branch and then I create a branch for a change, do a few commits, and then bring it back. But I’m confused by what “rewriting history” means. Why is that bad?

“Rewriting history” means changing existing commits that already exist in a repository: their IDs (hashes), parent relationships, or contents. In Git, a commit hash is derived from the commit’s content plus metadata (including its parent commit hash). If you change a commit’s parent (which is what rebase does), the commit hash changes even if the file changes are identical. Why that can be bad: - If a commit has been shared (pushed somewhere others fetch from, or you fetched it on another machine), rewriting it creates a new commit with a different hash. The old commit still exists in the other place, so now there are two versions of “the same” changes with different IDs. That can cause confusion and extra work to reconcile. - If you rebase a branch that someone else also has, their branch and your rebased branch no longer share the same commit IDs. Then Git sees them as diverged histories and you’ll have to merge or rebase again to reconcile. Why it can be good: - If the commits are only local (not shared), rewriting is harmless and can be helpful: you can reorder commits, squash them, edit messages, fixup mistakes, and keep the story tidy before you publish. So “bad” is contextual: rewriting shared history is risky; rewriting private/local history is often fine.

Okay. But I still don’t get the functional difference. Suppose I have main at commit A. I branch off and make commits B and C. Meanwhile main gets D and E. Now I want to integrate. What happens with merge vs rebase?

Great concrete example. Let’s name it: - main: A — D — E - feature: A — B — C 1) If you merge main into feature (or feature into main), Git creates a merge commit. Say you’re on feature and run: git merge main You’ll get: - feature: A — B — C — M where M is a merge commit with two parents: C and E. The history graph shows the branch divergence and merge. If you then merge feature into main: git checkout main git merge feature You might get another merge commit (or a fast-forward depending on exact state), but commonly: - main: A — D — E — (merge commit from feature) 2) If you rebase feature onto main: git checkout feature git rebase main Git will: - temporarily “remove” B and C from feature, - move feature’s base to E, - replay B then C as new commits B' and C' on top of E. Result: - main: A — D — E - feature: A — D — E — B' — C' Now feature contains new commits (B' and C') that are similar changes to B and C but with new hashes and new parents. Then integrating feature into main can be a fast-forward merge: git checkout main git merge feature Because main is behind feature and there’s no divergence now, Git can simply move main pointer to C' without creating a merge commit. So: merge preserves topology with merge commits; rebase linearizes by replaying commits.
That helps. But I often see people say “rebase your branch before merging” to avoid merge commits. Is that always a good idea?

Not always, but it’s common and can be good if used carefully. Pros of rebasing your branch onto main before merging: - Keeps history linear and easier to read with tools like git log. - Helps ensure your commits apply cleanly on top of the latest base; you resolve conflicts once during rebase rather than at merge time. - Often makes the final integration a fast-forward merge (no merge commit). Cons / caveats: - If your branch is already shared somewhere and others might have based work on it, rebasing will rewrite commit IDs and can create divergence for them. - If you rely on merge commits to preserve context like “these commits were part of one integration event,” linear history can lose that signal. (Some people like merge commits because they group changes.) A nuanced guideline: - Rebase to keep your own local branch up-to-date with main while you’re still working. - Once you’ve “published” the branch in a place you consider shared, avoid rebasing it unless you know no one else is using it, or you coordinate it carefully.
When you say “shared,” do you mean pushed to a remote? Because I push my branches to a remote just for backup between machines. Does that count?

It can count, but you can define your own safety boundary. If you push to a remote and then fetch/pull that branch elsewhere (another machine, another clone), rebasing after that will cause the two clones to disagree about commit IDs. It’s still solvable, but you’ll need to force-update the remote branch and then reconcile the other clone. If it’s just you using that remote branch, it’s not “dangerous,” but it is “history rewriting across clones,” which means: - You’ll likely need git push --force-with-lease after rebasing. - On the other clone, you’ll need to reset/rebase onto the updated remote branch. So: “shared” basically means “someone/another clone might have the old commit IDs and expect them to remain.” If the answer is yes, rebasing changes the rules.
I’ve had to use --force-with-lease and it scared me. What’s the difference between --force and --force-with-lease?

Both allow you to overwrite the remote branch pointer even if it’s not a fast-forward (which is what happens after rebase). - git push --force: unconditionally updates the remote branch to your local branch, even if the remote has new commits you don’t have. This can accidentally delete others’ new commits (or your own commits from another machine). - git push --force-with-lease: updates the remote branch only if the remote branch is still pointing to the commit you think it is (the “lease”). If the remote moved since you last fetched, the push is rejected. That’s a safety check against clobbering unseen updates. So if you ever must force-push, prefer --force-with-lease.
If I do rebase and force-push, what does the other clone do to catch up? I always get stuck with “diverged” messages.

Suppose clone1 rebases and force-pushes branch feature to remote, producing new commits B' and C'. Clone2 still has old B and C and maybe its local branch feature points there. On clone2, you’d do something like: 1) Fetch the updated remote state: git fetch origin 2) Decide what you want to keep: - If clone2 had no extra local commits on feature, easiest is to reset local feature to the remote: git checkout feature git reset --hard origin/feature That discards the old local branch history and points it to the new one. - If clone2 has local commits on top of the old history that you want to keep, you can rebase them onto the new remote branch: git checkout feature git rebase --onto origin/feature <old-base> feature But often simpler is: git checkout feature git rebase origin/feature If feature contains commits not in origin/feature, Git will attempt to replay them on top. Sometimes it’s easiest to create a backup pointer before doing anything: git branch feature-backup Then you can experiment safely. The “diverged” message typically happens because clone2’s feature and origin/feature have different commit histories that don’t fast-forward. Reset or rebase resolves it.
Okay. Switching gears: what about “merge main into feature” repeatedly while I’m working? I do that a lot because it avoids force pushing. Is that fine?

It’s fine technically. It’s a valid workflow: periodically merge main into your branch to bring it up to date. You’ll get merge commits on your branch, like: feature: A - B - C - M1 - ... - M2 where each M is a merge of main at that time. Tradeoffs: - Pros: no history rewriting; you can push freely without force; the branch records exactly when you integrated main. - Cons: history can get noisy with lots of merge commits; conflict resolution may happen multiple times; the final merge back to main may include these merges, making the graph more complex. Whether it’s “fine” depends on what you optimize for: simplicity and safety vs linear readability. A hybrid approach many people like: - While working locally: rebase feature onto main frequently (no merge commits). - When you need to publish without rewriting: either avoid rebasing after publishing, or accept force-push with lease for your own branches. But if you want to avoid force pushes entirely, merging main into your branch is the straightforward option.
I’m trying to understand conflicts. If I rebase, do I get more conflicts than merging? Or fewer?

It depends on timing and how often changes overlap, but here’s the typical pattern: - Rebase: you replay each commit one by one onto the new base. Conflicts can appear at the commit where the conflict is introduced. You resolve, continue, and potentially face another conflict on later commits. The benefit: conflicts are localized to the specific commit, which can be easier to reason about. - Merge: Git tries to combine the two branch tips in one go (three-way merge). You resolve conflicts once for the merge commit. The downside: the conflict resolution is for the combined set of changes; it can be harder to attribute which commit caused what. Sometimes rebase can cause you to resolve “similar” conflicts multiple times across commits; sometimes merge makes you resolve them once. But with careful commit structuring (small, focused commits), rebase conflict resolution is often manageable and sometimes clearer.
Can you show a concrete example? Like actual file changes that cause conflicts and how rebase vs merge changes the experience?

Sure. Imagine a file config.txt. At A (base): mode=fast timeout=30 On main, commits D and E happen: - D changes timeout to 45: mode=fast timeout=45 - E changes mode to safe: mode=safe timeout=45 On feature, you made B and C: - B changes timeout to 60: mode=fast timeout=60 - C adds a line retries=3: mode=fast timeout=60 retries=3 Now integrate. If you merge main into feature at C: Git tries to merge: - main tip: mode=safe, timeout=45 - feature tip: mode=fast, timeout=60, retries=3 Common ancestor A: mode=fast, timeout=30 It sees both changed mode (main changed to safe, feature kept fast but might be considered unchanged; actually feature didn’t change mode, so Git can take main’s change). For timeout, both changed it differently (45 vs 60), so conflict on timeout. retries line exists only on feature, so that’s fine. So you likely resolve one conflict (timeout) in the merge commit. If you rebase feature onto main: You replay B onto E first. When applying B (timeout=60) onto main’s file (mode=safe, timeout=45), conflict arises because B’s patch expects timeout=30 to be present and change it to 60, but base has 45. You resolve conflict for B, producing B' with timeout=60 on top of mode=safe. Then replay C (add retries=3). That applies cleanly. So rebase yields one conflict at commit B, then clean. In this case, same number of conflicts, but with rebase it’s tied to B specifically. In other cases, if multiple commits touch the same lines, rebase might make you resolve multiple times.
That makes sense. What about squashing? People talk about “squash and merge” vs “rebase and merge.” How do those relate?

They’re different knobs: - Squashing means combining multiple commits into one commit. You can do it via interactive rebase (git rebase -i) locally, or via “squash merge” (git merge --squash) when integrating. - Rebase means changing the base of commits (and optionally rewriting them) by replaying. Common patterns: 1) Rebase -i to squash/fixup/reorder your commits locally, then fast-forward merge: - You rewrite your branch history into a clean series (maybe 1 commit, maybe a few). - Then you merge it in (often fast-forward). 2) Merge --squash: - You don’t bring the branch’s commits as-is. Instead, you apply the net diff as a single new commit on the target branch. - The original branch commits remain on that branch, but the target branch only gets one commit representing the final state. Difference: - With rebase+squash, the resulting commit(s) are the rebased commits themselves (new hashes). - With merge --squash, Git doesn’t record a merge relationship; it just creates a normal commit with the combined changes. The history won’t show the branch structure at all.
If merge --squash doesn’t record a merge, does that cause problems later?

It can. Because there’s no merge commit linking the histories, Git doesn’t know that the branch’s commits were “integrated” in the ancestry sense. If you later try to merge that same branch again, Git may think none of its commits are in the target and can attempt to reapply changes, causing duplicates or conflicts. Typical safe usage: - Use squash merge for short-lived branches that won’t be merged again. After squashing, you usually abandon that branch. If you need to keep merging from a long-lived branch repeatedly, squash merges can be awkward because Git can’t track what was already merged via ancestry.
I’ve seen “rebase --onto” and it looks scary. When would I use that?

git rebase --onto is used when you want to move a subset of commits from one base to another, not necessarily the branch tip’s entire set. General form: git rebase --onto <newbase> <upstream> <branch> Meaning: take commits reachable from <branch> but not from <upstream>, and replay them onto <newbase>. Common use cases: - You branched from the wrong base and want to transplant your commits onto the correct base. - You want to split a branch: move some commits elsewhere while leaving others behind. - You want to drop a chunk of history. Example: main: A - D - E feature: A - B - C - F - G But you realize B and C were experimental and you only want F and G to be based on E. You could do: git rebase --onto main C feature This says: take commits after C (i.e., F and G) and replay them onto main (E). B and C stay behind (or you can drop them).
If I do interactive rebase, what exactly happens when I squash? Like what happens to commit messages and authors?

Interactive rebase rewrites commits by creating new commits. When you squash: - Git combines the changes from multiple commits into one new commit. - You get an editor prompt to combine/edit the commit message (unless you use fixup, which discards the squashed commit’s message). - Author/committer: - Author: typically preserved from the first commit in the squashed group (for the combined commit), though details can vary; committer becomes you at rebase time. - Committer date updates to the time of rewriting; author date may be preserved from original or set depending on options and tooling. If preserving exact authorship across multiple commits matters, squashing loses some granularity because those individual commits no longer exist in the rewritten history.
I want to get better at reading git graphs. Can you explain what “fast-forward” means and why it matters here?

A fast-forward merge happens when the target branch pointer can just move forward to the source branch tip without creating a merge commit, because the target is an ancestor of the source. Example: main: A - D - E feature: A - D - E - B' - C' Here main (E) is directly behind feature (C') with no divergence. So merging feature into main is just: main pointer moves from E to C'. No merge commit needed. Fast-forward matters because: - It keeps history linear (no merge commit). - It’s only possible when there’s no divergence. Rebase is often used to make fast-forward possible by removing divergence (replaying feature commits on top of the latest main).
Sometimes I run git pull and it creates a merge commit. Other times it rebases. What’s happening there?

git pull is basically: 1) git fetch 2) then integrate fetched changes into your current branch The integration step depends on configuration and flags: - Default (often): merge git pull = fetch + merge If your local branch and remote-tracking branch diverged, it creates a merge commit. - With --rebase: git pull --rebase = fetch + rebase It rebases your local commits on top of the fetched remote commits. - With config: git config pull.rebase true (or false) git config branch.<name>.rebase true So the same command git pull can behave differently depending on settings.
Is pull --rebase safe? It feels like it rewrites history too.

It rewrites your local commits (the ones you made that aren’t on the remote yet) by replaying them on top of the updated remote. That’s generally safe, because it doesn’t rewrite the remote history; it rewrites only your local unpublished commits. However, if you had already pushed those commits to the remote (so they are on the remote branch), then doing pull --rebase and then pushing can require a force push (because you rewrote commits that were already published). So it’s “safe” when used in the intended scenario: you have local commits, remote advanced, and you want to replay your local commits on top.
What about tags? If I rebase, do tags move?

Tags point to specific commits. Rebasing creates new commits; the old commits still exist (until garbage collection), but your branch pointer moves to the new commits. - Lightweight tags: just refs to commit hashes. They do not automatically move when you rebase, because they still point to the old commit hash. - Annotated tags: also point to commits (with extra metadata). Same behavior: they don’t move automatically. If you tagged a commit on your branch and then rebase, the tag will still reference the old commit, which might no longer be on the branch. You can move tags manually (delete and recreate, or force-update), but that’s another form of rewriting references.
I’ve also heard about “reflog” saving you if you mess up. How does that relate to rebase?

reflog records where your branch references (like HEAD) have pointed recently. Since rebase moves branch pointers and creates new commits, reflog is often what lets you recover old states. Example: you do a rebase and regret it. You can inspect: git reflog You’ll see entries like: HEAD@{0}: rebase finished: returning to refs/heads/feature HEAD@{1}: rebase: commit message... HEAD@{n}: checkout: moving from ... If you want to go back to the pre-rebase state, you can reset to the old HEAD: git reset --hard HEAD@{<index>} Or create a branch at that point: git branch feature-before-rebase HEAD@{<index>} So reflog makes rebasing much less scary because you can usually undo it as long as the old commits haven’t been garbage-collected.
Can you walk me through “git rebase --abort” and “--continue” steps? I always forget.

During a rebase, Git applies commits one at a time. If a conflict occurs, it stops and tells you to resolve it. The typical loop: 1) Start rebase: git rebase main 2) Conflict happens. Git says: fix conflicts, then run: git status will show unmerged paths. 3) Resolve conflicts by editing files, then stage them: git add <files> 4) Continue: git rebase --continue Git will create the rebased version of the current commit and move to the next. If you realize you don’t want to do this rebase at all: - Abort: git rebase --abort This returns your branch to the exact state it was in before the rebase began. There’s also: - Skip the current patch (drops that commit): git rebase --skip Useful if you decide that commit is unnecessary or already applied another way, but be careful: it changes content.
If I do merge and get conflicts, the commands are different, right?

Yes. For a merge conflict: 1) You run: git merge otherbranch and it stops with conflicts. 2) Resolve conflicts, then stage: git add <files> 3) Complete the merge by making the merge commit: git commit (Some Git setups auto-open an editor; sometimes it auto-commits if configured, but usually you commit manually.) If you want to abandon the merge attempt: git merge --abort So rebase uses --continue/--abort as well, but merge uses commit to finish (unless auto-commit) and merge --abort to bail out.
Let’s talk about “clean history.” People say rebase makes history clean. But isn’t that kind of fake? It pretends we didn’t branch.

It is a curated view. Whether that’s “fake” or “clean” depends on your goal for history. Git history can serve multiple purposes: - Forensic record: exactly what happened and when branches diverged and were merged. - Narrative / change log: a readable series of logical changes that led to the current state. - Bisectability: ability to run tests at each commit and find regressions. A linear history from rebase often improves narrative and bisectability (if commits are well-formed), but it hides the exact branching structure. Merge commits preserve structure and can preserve context like “these commits were integrated together.” Neither is objectively correct; they emphasize different properties.
You mentioned bisectability. How does rebase help with git bisect?

git bisect walks commit-by-commit to find the first bad commit. It works best when: - Each commit builds (or at least is in a runnable state). - The commit history is not cluttered with lots of “merge noise” that doesn’t represent meaningful changes. Rebase can help by: - Encouraging smaller, coherent commits that are in good order. - Removing merge commits that might introduce complex states where the merge commit itself is the first bad commit, even though neither side individually is bad (e.g., integration issues). But merge commits can also be meaningful: if a bug is introduced by interaction of changes from two branches, the merge commit is the correct “first bad” commit. So removing merge commits can sometimes make it harder to pinpoint integration-introduced issues. It’s a tradeoff.
Is there a way to keep merge commits but still have linear-ish history?

You can use a strategy like: - Merge with --no-ff to always create a merge commit even when fast-forward is possible, which preserves a “grouping” commit. But that’s the opposite of linear. If you want linear main history but keep some notion of grouping, you can: - Rebase topic branches to be clean, then use a merge commit only at integration time (optional). Or: - Use annotated tags or notes to mark integration points. There’s also: git log --first-parent This shows the mainline progression through merge commits, hiding the details of merged branches unless you dive in. That can make a non-linear history easier to read without rewriting.
I’ve seen “git merge --rebase” somewhere, is that real?

The common thing is “git pull --rebase.” For merge, there isn’t a “merge --rebase” in standard Git because merge and rebase are different operations. There is a merge strategy option --rebase in some tooling wrappers, but in core Git, you typically choose one: merge or rebase. What you might be thinking of: - git rebase --rebase-merges (or -r), which attempts to preserve merge commits while rebasing. That’s a more advanced form of rebase that tries to replay merges too.
rebase-merges sounds advanced. What problem does it solve?

Normal rebase flattens merges: if your branch contains merge commits (for example, you merged another branch into your branch), a standard rebase will usually linearize and drop the merge structure, replaying commits in a linear sequence. git rebase --rebase-merges tries to preserve the branching/merge structure within the rebased range. It can be useful if: - You have a branch with meaningful merges inside it (like you combined sub-branches) and you want to move the whole structure onto a new base without flattening it. But it’s more complex, can be harder to resolve conflicts, and can surprise you if you’re not expecting the graph to be reconstructed. Most of the time, people keep it simple: avoid internal merges on a topic branch, or accept flattening.
Let’s say I prefer rebase. What should I set in my git config to make things less annoying?
