Common helpful settings if you like rebase-oriented workflows: 1) Make git pull rebase by default: git config --global pull.rebase true This makes pull do fetch + rebase for branches where it applies. 2) Auto-stash during rebase/pull if you have local uncommitted changes: git config --global rebase.autoStash true Then git pull --rebase can temporarily stash and reapply your working tree changes. 3) Reuse recorded conflict resolutions (can be very helpful): git config --global rerere.enabled true Git will remember how you resolved a conflict and try to apply the same resolution next time. 4) Make pushes safer: Use --force-with-lease when needed; there isn’t a config that forces it for all pushes in vanilla Git, but you can make an alias: git config --global alias.pushf "push --force-with-lease"
I’ve heard rerere but never used it. How does it work in practice?

rerere (“reuse recorded resolution”) records how you resolved a specific conflict (based on the conflict hunks). If the same conflict appears again later (common when you repeatedly rebase or when you cherry-pick a series), Git can auto-apply your previous resolution. Practical flow: - Enable it: git config rerere.enabled true - When a conflict happens, you resolve it as usual and stage the files. - Git records the preimage (conflict state) and the resolution. - Next time Git sees the same conflict pattern, it can resolve it automatically or partially, reducing manual work. It’s especially useful with rebase because similar conflicts can arise across multiple commits or repeated rebases.
Is cherry-pick basically like rebase?

They’re related but not the same. - cherry-pick takes one (or a range of) commit(s) and applies them onto your current branch, creating new commit(s) with new hashes. - rebase takes a series of commits and replays them onto a new base, effectively a batch of cherry-picks in order. So conceptually, rebase = repeated cherry-picks + moving branch pointer, with extra features like interactive editing.
Suppose I accidentally merged main into my feature branch, but I wanted a linear history. Can I “undo” that merge and rebase instead?

Yes, often. Scenario: your feature branch has a merge commit M that merged main into feature. If you haven’t pushed (or you’re okay rewriting), you can reset to before the merge and rebase. Steps: 1) Find the commit before the merge. If M is the merge commit at HEAD: git log --oneline --decorate or use reflog: git reflog 2) Reset feature to the pre-merge commit (say it was C): git reset --hard C 3) Rebase onto main: git rebase main If you already pushed the merge commit and don’t want to rewrite, you can still rebase future commits, but removing the merge commit would require rewriting history. Alternatively, if the merge commit is not at the tip and you want to excise it, interactive rebase can drop it (but dropping a merge commit is more complicated; standard interactive rebase doesn’t handle merges unless you use --rebase-merges).
Wait, interactive rebase can’t edit merge commits?

By default, interactive rebase works on a linear series of commits. Merge commits complicate that because they have two parents. Standard rebase generally ignores merge commits in the todo list (it linearizes). If you want to preserve and manipulate merges, you use: git rebase -i --rebase-merges <upstream> Then the todo list includes commands like “merge” to recreate merges. But if your goal is to remove the merge commit and linearize, a normal rebase might already flatten it (depending on where you start), though results can be surprising. For simple “I merged main into my branch once and want to get rid of that merge commit,” often the cleanest is: reset before the merge and rebase.
I think I’m getting it. But I still don’t know what to do day-to-day. Can you propose a consistent set of practices that avoids the worst pitfalls?

Here’s a consistent approach that’s technically sound and avoids most pitfalls, without referencing any particular hosting platform: 1) Keep main as the stable base. - Don’t rewrite main history once it’s published. 2) For a short-lived feature branch: - Make small, meaningful commits. - Periodically update it with main using rebase (preferred for linearity): git fetch origin git rebase origin/main Resolve conflicts as needed. 3) Before integrating your feature into main: - Optionally run interactive rebase to clean up commits: git rebase -i origin/main Use squash/fixup/reword to make the history readable. - Then integrate via fast-forward merge if possible: git checkout main git merge --ff-only feature If --ff-only fails, it means main moved; rebase feature onto the updated main and try again. 4) If you must keep the remote feature branch updated and you rebased: - Push with: git push --force-with-lease 5) If you don’t want to force push at all: - Don’t rebase after pushing; instead merge main into feature occasionally: git merge origin/main This will keep the remote branch always fast-forwardable to your local branch without rewriting. Pick one of (4) or (5) depending on your comfort with force-with-lease.
What does --ff-only do exactly?

git merge --ff-only refuses to create a merge commit. It will only perform the merge if it can be done as a fast-forward. So it’s a safety check: - If your main is behind feature and there’s no divergence, it fast-forwards. - If there’s divergence, it aborts instead of creating a merge commit. It’s useful if you want to enforce linear history on that branch.
Sometimes I see “Already up to date” after merge. But when I rebase it says “Current branch is up to date.” Are those the same?

Yes, essentially. Both mean there were no new commits to integrate from the specified branch into your current branch. - merge: “Already up to date.” means your current branch already contains the commits from the other branch (the other branch tip is an ancestor of current). - rebase: “Current branch is up to date.” means your branch’s base already includes the upstream commits; there’s nothing to replay.
How do I visualize what rebase will do before I do it?

A few ways: 1) Compare commit lists: git log --oneline --graph --decorate main..feature Shows commits in feature that aren’t in main. git log --oneline --graph --decorate feature..main Shows commits in main that aren’t in feature. 2) Find the merge base: git merge-base main feature This tells you the common ancestor commit. Rebase typically moves the feature commits after that base onto main’s tip. 3) Dry-run style: There’s no perfect “dry-run” for rebase that shows exact conflicts, but you can: - create a temporary branch pointer and try it: git checkout feature git branch feature-backup git rebase main If it’s messy, you can abort or reset to backup.
Is there any difference in the resulting code between merge and rebase?

If both operations succeed and you resolve conflicts equivalently, the final snapshot (the content of the files at the tip) can be identical. The difference is in history structure and commit IDs. However, there are cases where the resulting code can differ: - If conflict resolutions differ (because you resolve at different times or contexts). - If your rebase applies commits in a different order than a merge would combine them (rare unless there are complex dependencies or if you reorder commits). - If you use options like --no-ff merges vs fast-forward. But typically, for simple cases, the end state is the same; only history differs.
I’m curious about the internal model. Why does rebase change hashes? You said the parent hash is part of it. Can you explain that more concretely?

A Git commit object includes: - A pointer to a tree object (snapshot of the directory) - Parent commit hash(es) (one parent for normal commit, two for merge commit) - Author name/email/time - Committer name/email/time - Commit message The commit hash is computed from the content of the commit object (plus a header). So if you change any part—especially the parent hash—you change the commit hash. Rebase changes the parent relationship: B originally had parent A; after rebasing onto E, B' has parent E. That alone changes the commit content and thus the hash, even if the file changes are identical.
If hashes change, how does Git know it’s “the same change” when rebasing?

It doesn’t inherently know it’s “the same” in an identity sense; it just applies patches. During rebase, Git computes the diff introduced by each commit (relative to its parent) and then applies that diff onto the new base. If it applies cleanly, you get a new commit with similar changes. Git can sometimes detect that a commit was already applied upstream via “patch-id” equivalence (especially during rebase/merge/cherry-pick operations) if the patch content matches, but that’s heuristic. The new commit is still a different object with a different hash.
What about “git rebase --onto main” vs “git rebase main”? I see both.

Most of the time you use: git rebase <upstream> Example: on feature, git rebase main This means: take the current branch’s commits that are not in main, and replay them onto main. git rebase --onto is for specifying a different new base and/or a different upstream boundary. If you just want to rebase your current branch onto main, you don’t need --onto.
Let’s talk about “linear history” vs “true history.” Is there a way to keep both?

One way conceptually is: - Keep a linearized main branch (via rebase before integrating, or squash merges). - Keep the original branch history elsewhere (the branch ref itself, or tags pointing to pre-rewrite commits), at least temporarily. But if you truly want both permanently, you’d need to keep references to the original commits (like not deleting old branch refs, or tagging the pre-rebase tip). Otherwise, once refs are removed and garbage collection runs, the old commits can be pruned. So yes, you can keep both if you keep refs to both histories. But that’s extra management.
If I rebase and then later realize I lost some commits, how do I find them?

Use reflog and dangling commits. 1) reflog: git reflog show feature or just: git reflog Look for the old HEAD before the rebase started. You can create a branch there: git branch recovered <hash> 2) fsck for dangling objects (more advanced): git fsck --lost-found This can find dangling commits not referenced by any ref. But reflog is usually easier. Also, before doing risky operations, creating a backup branch pointer is simple: git branch backup-before-rebase Then you can always return.
Does rebase affect commit signatures (GPG/SSH signing)?

Yes. If commits are signed, rebasing recreates commits, so: - The old signatures remain on the old commits. - The new commits created by rebase will not be signed unless you sign them during rebase. You can configure Git to sign commits by default: git config commit.gpgsign true Then commits created during rebase will be signed as they’re created (assuming your signing setup works non-interactively or you can provide passphrase as needed). If you rely on existing signatures, rebasing invalidates that chain because the signed objects are different.
Interesting. Another thing: sometimes after a rebase, my branch shows the same commits but with different hashes, and then when I merge I get duplicates. How does that happen?

Duplicates usually happen when the same changes exist as different commits on both sides, and Git can’t detect they’re equivalent. Common scenario: - You had commits B and C on feature. - You merged feature into main (so B and C are now in main). - Later, you rebase feature (which still had B and C) onto main, producing B' and C'. Now main has B and C, and feature has B' and C'. When you merge, Git sees B' and C' as new commits and may apply them again, resulting in duplicate changes or conflicts. Avoiding this: - Don’t rebase a branch after it has been merged into the base branch (unless you’re doing some special cleanup and you know what you’re doing). - If you need to continue work after merging, start a new branch from main, or reset feature to main after merge: git checkout feature git reset --hard main So feature doesn’t still contain the old commits.
That’s a good point. So after I merge a feature branch into main, should I delete the branch?

Often yes, especially for short-lived branches. Deleting the branch pointer prevents accidentally continuing on it and reintroducing old commits. If you want to keep the branch name for future work, you can “recreate” it at main: git branch -f feature main (or delete and re-create) This makes feature point to main’s tip, so it’s a clean starting point.
What about “git switch” vs “git checkout”? Does it matter for this?

Not conceptually for merge vs rebase. git switch is newer and clearer for changing branches, while git checkout is older and does multiple jobs (switch branches and restore files). Equivalent examples: - git checkout feature - git switch feature For creating a branch: - git checkout -b feature - git switch -c feature You can use whichever you prefer; it doesn’t change how rebase/merge behave.
Can you show me a sample session with commands for both approaches, including what the log might look like?

Sure. I’ll outline two sessions with simplified logs. Assume starting state: main: A You branch feature at A. You do: git switch -c feature # commit B # commit C Meanwhile main gets D and E (in another place). You fetch: git switch main git pull Now main: A - D - E You switch back: git switch feature Feature: A - B - C Approach 1: Merge main into feature, then merge feature into main On feature: git merge main This creates merge commit M. Graph: main:    A - D - E feature: A - B - C - M \ / E Then integrate into main: git switch main git merge feature Depending on whether main moved, you might get a merge commit N: main: A - D - E - N \   / B-C-M Log might show merges. Approach 2: Rebase feature onto main, then fast-forward merge On feature: git rebase main Now feature becomes: main:    A - D - E feature: A - D - E - B' - C' Then: git switch main git merge --ff-only feature main fast-forwards to C'. Log is linear: A, D, E, B', C'.
In approach 2, what happens to the old B and C commits?

They’re no longer referenced by the feature branch pointer after the rebase. But they typically remain in the repository as orphaned objects for a while, reachable via reflog. Over time, Git’s garbage collection may prune them if nothing references them and their reflog entries expire. So they’re not immediately “deleted,” but they’re not part of the branch history anymore.
I keep hearing “never rebase main.” Why is that a hard rule?

Because main is usually treated as a shared, stable reference point. Rebasing it would rewrite commit IDs for commits that many other refs may depend on (branches, tags, clones). That creates large-scale divergence, requiring everyone/other clones to reconcile by resetting, and it can break references like tags, external pointers to commit hashes, etc. Even if you personally can force-push it, rewriting a widely used base branch causes disproportionate disruption. That’s why it’s a strong rule: don’t rewrite published base branches.
If I want a linear history on main but also avoid rewriting main, what’s the best path?

Rebase the feature branch before it gets integrated, not main. Workflow: - Keep main moving forward normally. - For your feature branch, rebase it onto the latest main (rewriting only feature commits). - Then integrate feature into main with fast-forward (or with a merge commit if you prefer, but you can still keep the commits linear). That way, main’s existing history is never rewritten, but the commits you add to main are already linearized.
I sometimes do “git merge --no-ff” intentionally. How does that interact with rebasing?

--no-ff forces a merge commit even when a fast-forward is possible. If your feature branch is rebased onto main and could be fast-forwarded, --no-ff will still create a merge commit, preserving a visible “integration point.” So you could: - rebase feature to clean up its commits, - then merge --no-ff to keep a merge commit as a marker. Result: history is not purely linear (it has a merge commit), but the feature branch commits are still based directly on main and the merge commit groups them.
Does that defeat the purpose of rebase?

It depends on the purpose. If your only purpose is “no merge commits ever,” then yes, it defeats that. If your purpose is “avoid messy interleaving and keep commits in a good order,” rebase still helps. The merge commit becomes a single, intentional marker rather than a bunch of incidental merges from keeping branches up to date.
What about “octopus merges” and other merge strategies? Are those relevant?

They’re more advanced and less common in day-to-day workflows. - Octopus merge is merging more than two branches at once: git merge branch1 branch2 branch3 Git can do it automatically if there are no conflicts. It creates a merge commit with multiple parents. This is generally not something you’d pair with rebase for typical feature integration. Rebase is about linearizing one branch onto another; octopus merges are about combining multiple lines at once. Other strategies like ours/theirs, recursive, etc., matter when resolving conflicts, but they don’t change the basic rebase vs merge tradeoffs.
I’ve used “git merge -s ours” once. What’s that?

git merge -s ours is a merge strategy that records a merge commit but keeps the current branch’s content (it ignores the other branch’s changes in the resulting tree). It’s used in special cases, like marking a branch as merged without actually taking its changes, often to prevent future merges from reintroducing changes. It’s not a typical integration strategy for normal development, but it illustrates that merge commits can be used as metadata: they affect ancestry even if the tree content doesn’t incorporate changes.
That’s wild. Back to basics: how do I decide between rebase and merge in my case? I’m one person using git across a couple machines, with a remote as a sync point.

In that setup, you can choose either, but here’s a practical decision: If you value a clean, linear history and you’re okay with occasionally force-updating your remote feature branches: - Rebase your feature branch onto main as you go. - Push with --force-with-lease when needed. - On the other machine, fetch and reset/rebase to catch up. If you want to minimize force pushes and keep syncing simple: - Merge main into your feature branch when you need updates. - Push normally (no force). - Merge feature into main at the end (possibly with a merge commit). A compromise: - Rebase locally as much as you want. - Only push when you’re ready, and avoid rewriting after you’ve pushed (or accept force-with-lease).
If I do rebase and force-push, can I lose commits on the remote accidentally?

With --force, yes, easily. With --force-with-lease, it’s much harder, but still possible if you misunderstand what you’re overwriting. The safest habits: - Always fetch before force-with-lease, so your “lease” is up to date. - Use --force-with-lease, not --force. - Consider pushing rebased work to a different branch name if you want to preserve the old remote branch temporarily. Also, remember: even if you overwrite the remote branch pointer, the old commits might still exist on the remote for some time, but you shouldn’t rely on that.
Can you explain what “git pull --rebase” does with merge commits on my local branch?

If your local branch contains merge commits that are not on the remote, pull --rebase can get complicated because rebasing merge commits is not straightforward (it may flatten them or skip them, depending). In many cases, if your local branch has merge commits, pull --rebase will: - attempt to rebase the linear commits, - and may refuse or behave unexpectedly if there are local merges. That’s why, if you prefer rebasing, it’s often recommended to avoid creating merge commits on your local topic branches in the first place (use rebase instead of merging updates), so pull --rebase stays simple. If you do need to rebase a branch with merges, you might need: git pull --rebase=merges or: git rebase --rebase-merges depending on Git version and configuration.
How do I check my Git version and see if it supports those options?

Run: git --version Then you can check help pages: git help pull git help rebase Search for “rebase-merges” and pull’s rebase modes. Git has evolved; options like --rebase-merges have improved over versions.
Another confusion: If I rebase feature onto main, and then main changes again, do I rebase again? Doesn’t that keep rewriting my commits over and over?

Yes, rebasing repeatedly rewrites your feature commits each time, producing new hashes each time. Locally, that’s fine. It’s basically “keep moving my work on top of the latest base.” It becomes a problem only if: - you’ve published those commits somewhere and other clones rely on their hashes. If it’s just you and you’re comfortable force-pushing, it’s still okay, but it can be annoying. Ways to reduce repeated rewriting: - Rebase less frequently; only rebase when you need to incorporate base changes or right before integrating. - Use merge updates instead of rebase updates if you want stable commit IDs on the remote branch.
Is there a concept of “stable” commits vs “draft” commits?

Not built into Git, but conceptually: - Draft commits: local commits you expect to rewrite (via rebase -i, amend, squash). - Stable commits: commits you consider published/final and won’t rewrite. A useful habit is to treat anything you’ve pushed to a shared remote branch as stable, unless you intentionally plan to force-update it.
I often use “git commit --amend” to fix the last commit. Is that also rewriting history?

Yes. Amend replaces the last commit with a new commit (new hash), because it changes commit content and/or metadata. So it’s safe for local unpublished commits, but if the commit was already pushed and others/clones depend on it, amending requires force push and can cause divergence.
What’s the difference between amending and interactive rebase to edit a commit?

Functionally similar in that both rewrite commits. - git commit --amend: edits the most recent commit (HEAD). - git rebase -i: can edit older commits (not just HEAD), reorder, squash, etc. During interactive rebase, you can mark a commit as “edit,” Git stops there, and you can amend it, then continue. So amend is a quick shortcut for “edit HEAD.”
Let’s say I want to keep a clean series of commits, each one builds. How do I structure my work with rebase to achieve that?

A good approach: 1) Make small commits for logical units of change. 2) If you make a “fixup” commit (like addressing a linter error or a missed file), keep it as a separate commit temporarily. 3) Before integrating, run: git rebase -i main Then: - reorder commits to put prerequisites first - squash fixups into their intended commits (use “fixup” command) - reword messages for clarity 4) Optionally, run tests after rebase to ensure the rewritten sequence is good. This turns your messy working history into a coherent, bisect-friendly series.
I’ve seen “git commit --fixup” and “--autosquash.” How do those work?

They’re designed to streamline interactive rebase. - git commit --fixup <commit> Creates a commit whose message indicates it should be squashed into the specified commit (it prefixes with “fixup!”). - Then run rebase with autosquash: git rebase -i --autosquash main Git automatically rearranges and marks fixup commits to be squashed into the target commit, so you don’t manually move lines around. There’s also: git commit --squash <commit> which includes the commit message in a “squash!” message to help combine messages.
Nice. Does autosquash work if I rebase onto main that has moved?

Yes, because autosquash is about arranging commits within the rebased range. As long as the commit you’re fixing up is in the range you’re rebasing (and you reference it correctly), it will place fixup commits next to their targets and mark them appropriately. If the target commit isn’t in the range (e.g., it’s already in main, and you’re fixing it from a different branch), then fixup won’t apply in the same way. Fixups are most useful within your local series.
I think I want a “decision tree.” Like: if X, do rebase; if Y, do merge. Can you give me that?

Here’s a simple decision tree: 1) Are you integrating changes from one branch into another? - Yes → go to 2 2) Do you need to preserve the true branching structure (merge commits meaningful), or avoid rewriting? - Yes → use merge - No → go to 3 3) Have the commits you’re about to rewrite been published in a place where another clone might rely on them? - Yes → prefer merge (or avoid rewriting; if you must rewrite, expect force-with-lease and resets elsewhere) - No → go to 4 4) Do you want a linear history and/or to clean up commits (squash, reorder, edit)? - Yes → use rebase (possibly interactive) - No → merge is fine And for updating a topic branch with main: - Want linear + okay rewriting local commits → rebase - Want stable commit IDs + no force pushes → merge main into your branch
Can you explain what happens to the commit graph if I do “merge feature into main” vs “rebase feature onto main then merge”? Like, what does git log --graph show?

If you merge feature into main without rebasing (and there was divergence), you’ll see a branch in the graph: *   N (merge commit) |\ | * C | * B * | E * | D |/ * A If you rebase feature onto main first and then fast-forward merge, graph is linear: * C' * B' * E * D * A So --graph becomes much simpler.
Is linear always simpler? Sometimes I like knowing that B and C were done before D and E.

Linear history after rebase will show B' and C' after D and E, even if you originally did B and C earlier. That’s one of the “it’s a curated narrative” aspects. If chronological order matters, you can use: git log --date-order or view author dates vs committer dates. Rebase changes committer dates (and often the visual ordering) because commits are recreated. So linear history prioritizes “applied on top of base” order rather than “originally created” order.
That’s important. So rebase changes dates?

It typically changes the committer date because you are creating new commits now. The author date may remain the original author date (Git preserves it by default), but the committer date updates. So you can still see when you originally authored changes vs when they were applied/rewritten.
Okay. Another question: If I have a local branch and I rebase it, will my uncommitted changes be affected?

Rebase operates on commits, but it needs a clean working tree to safely apply patches. If you have uncommitted changes: - Git will usually refuse to start a rebase and ask you to commit or stash. - If you have rebase.autoStash enabled, Git can automatically stash them before rebasing and reapply afterward. But if the rebase introduces conflicts that overlap your uncommitted changes, the stash apply can conflict too. So safest is to commit or stash manually before rebasing.
I’ve had stash conflicts too. Is there a better way than stashing?

Options: - Make a temporary “WIP” commit (even if you plan to squash it later): git add -A git commit -m "WIP" Then rebase. Later you can squash/fixup or reset. - Use git worktree to have multiple working directories for different branches, so you can switch branches without stashing. Example: git worktree add ../repo-feature feature Now you have a separate directory checked out to feature; you can update main elsewhere without touching uncommitted changes. worktree is a powerful alternative to stashing if you frequently switch contexts.
worktree sounds useful. Does it change anything about rebase/merge?

Not conceptually. It just gives you multiple working directories tied to the same repository object database. You can rebase in one worktree and merge in another. It reduces friction around uncommitted changes.
Coming back to rebase vs merge: is there any performance difference?

Usually negligible for typical repositories. For very large histories: - Rebase can be more work because it applies commits one by one. - Merge does one merge operation. But Git is efficient, and the choice is usually driven by history semantics, not performance. Conflict resolution time dominates anyway.
How do I handle a situation where rebase keeps failing because of conflicts, and I just want to get it done?

If rebase conflicts are too painful, merging might be simpler because you resolve conflicts once. You can: 1) Abort the rebase: git rebase --abort 2) Merge main into your branch: git merge main Resolve conflicts once, commit the merge. Or: - If you still want a linear history but rebase is hard because commits are too granular and conflict repeatedly, you can squash your branch first (locally) into one commit, then rebase that single commit: git reset --soft <base> git commit -m "combined change" git rebase main But that discards intermediate commit granularity. Another tactic: - Enable rerere and let it learn resolutions over time.
Wait, git reset --soft <base> then commit—what does that do?

Suppose your feature branch started at A and has commits B and C. If you do: git reset --soft A It moves HEAD and the branch pointer back to A but keeps the changes from B and C staged in the index (as if you had applied them but not committed). Then: git commit -m "combined change" creates a single commit that contains the net effect of B and C. It’s a way to squash without interactive rebase, but you must be careful to pick the correct base commit.
That’s a neat trick. But it also rewrites history, right?
Yes. It replaces B and C with a new commit. Safe for local/unpublished history; otherwise it requires force push.
If I want to learn this deeply, what mental model should I adopt? I still mix up “branch” vs “commit.”

A good mental model: - A commit is a snapshot + pointers to parent(s). - A branch is just a movable pointer (a ref) to a commit. - HEAD points to the currently checked-out branch (or directly to a commit in detached HEAD state). - Rebase moves a branch pointer by creating new commits that have different parents. - Merge creates a new commit that has multiple parents, and moves the current branch pointer to it. If you remember “branches are pointers,” a lot of Git behavior becomes clearer. Rebase = copy commits to new place and move pointer. Merge = create join commit and move pointer.
What is detached HEAD and does it matter for rebase/merge?

Detached HEAD means HEAD points directly to a commit, not to a branch ref. You can still inspect, build, and even commit, but new commits won’t be on a named branch unless you create one. Rebase and merge are usually done on branches. If you’re detached, you can’t easily “move a branch pointer” because you’re not on one. Git will typically prevent or complicate operations. If you find yourself detached and want to keep work: git switch -c new-branch-name to attach your current commit to a branch.
I think I’ve got the conceptual part. But I still want a concrete recommendation: should I default to rebase or merge for updating my feature branch?

If your priority is learning and having a readable history, default to: - rebase your feature branch onto main while you work (especially if you haven’t published those commits widely), - and use --force-with-lease when pushing rebased updates to your remote sync branch. If your priority is minimizing “sharp edges” and never force-pushing, default to: - merge main into your feature branch to update it. Given you said you use a remote as a sync point across machines, the deciding factor is how comfortable you are with force-with-lease and occasionally resetting on the other machine. If that sounds manageable, rebase is a good default; otherwise merge is the smoother sync experience.
I’m leaning rebase, but I’m worried I’ll mess up and lose work. Can you give me a safe step-by-step “rebasing a feature branch onto main” checklist?

Checklist that keeps you safe: 1) Ensure working tree is clean: git status If not clean, commit or stash. 2) Make a backup branch pointer: git branch backup/feature-before-rebase 3) Fetch latest main: git fetch origin 4) Rebase onto updated main: git rebase origin/main 5) If conflicts: - resolve files - git add <files> - git rebase --continue Repeat until done. If it becomes too messy: git rebase --abort and you’re back to start. 6) Run tests/build if applicable. 7) Push updated branch: git push --force-with-lease origin feature If anything goes wrong, you can always: git reset --hard backup/feature-before-rebase or use reflog to find prior states.
Perfect. Last question (for now): what’s the biggest misconception people have about rebase vs merge?

Probably this: “Rebase is dangerous; merge is safe.” The real distinction is: - Rebase is dangerous when used on commits that others/clones depend on, because it rewrites commit IDs and requires force-updating references. - Rebase is very safe and powerful for local history editing and for keeping a branch up to date before integrating. And conversely, merge isn’t always “safe” in the sense of “no problems”: it can create complex histories and can cause tricky conflict resolution too. It’s just non-rewriting, which reduces a class of coordination issues. If you internalize “rebase rewrites commits; merge preserves commits,” and then decide based on whether rewriting is acceptable for the commits in question, you’ll make consistent choices.
Thanks. I’ll try the checklist next time and see how it goes. If I run into a weird state, I’ll ask about it.

Sounds good. If you do run into a weird state, the most helpful info is: the exact commands you ran and the output of git status and a small git log --oneline --graph --decorate --all snippet. That usually makes it immediately clear whether you should abort, continue, reset, or create a recovery branch.

I have a slow SQL query. It’s a JOIN taking ~8 seconds on ~2M rows. I want help optimizing it with indexing. Here’s the query and EXPLAIN output. Query (PostgreSQL 14): SELECT o.id, o.created_at, c.email, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = o.id WHERE o.created_at >= '2024-01-01' AND o.status IN ('paid', 'shipped') AND c.country = 'US' GROUP BY o.id, o.created_at, c.email ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 50; Tables: orders: ~2,000,000 rows order_items: ~12,000,000 rows customers: ~300,000 rows Indexes: orders: PK(id), idx_orders_customer_id(customer_id), idx_orders_created_at(created_at) order_items: PK(id), idx_order_items_order_id(order_id) customers: PK(id), idx_customers_country(country) EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS): Limit  (cost=... rows=50) (actual time=8000..8000 rows=50 loops=1) ->  Sort  (actual time=7999..7999 rows=50 loops=1) Sort Key: o.created_at DESC Sort Method: top-N heapsort  Memory: 70kB ->  HashAggregate (actual time=7800..7950 rows=400000 loops=1) Group Key: o.id, o.created_at, c.email ->  Hash Join (actual time=1200..7000 rows=12000000 loops=1) Hash Cond: (oi.order_id = o.id) ->  Seq Scan on order_items oi (actual time=0.02..2200 rows=12000000 loops=1) ->  Hash  (actual time=1150..1150 rows=900000 loops=1) ->  Hash Join (actual time=300..900 rows=900000 loops=1) Hash Cond: (o.customer_id = c.id) ->  Bitmap Heap Scan on orders o (actual time=50..400 rows=1500000 loops=1) Recheck Cond: (created_at >= '2024-01-01'::date) Filter: (status = ANY ('{paid,shipped}'::text[])) ->  Bitmap Index Scan on idx_orders_created_at (actual time=40..40 rows=1600000 loops=1) Index Cond: (created_at >= '2024-01-01'::date) ->  Hash  (actual time=240..240 rows=100000 loops=1) ->  Bitmap Heap Scan on customers c (actual time=20..200 rows=100000 loops=1) Recheck Cond: (country = 'US'::text) ->  Bitmap Index Scan on idx_customers_country (actual time=10..10 rows=100000 loops=1) Index Cond: (country = 'US'::text) Buffers: shared hit=... read=... What should I change? Indexes? Query rewrite?

The plan shows two main cost drivers: 1) Full sequential scan of order_items (12M rows) and hashing/joining them, even though you only need aggregates for the “top 50 most recent orders” after filtering orders/customers. The planner is aggregating across a huge join result (12M rows) and only then sorting/limiting. Ideally you want to: - First identify the 50 most recent qualifying orders (after filtering by date/status and customer country), - Then join order_items for just those 50 orders and aggregate. 2) The current grouping includes c.email, which forces the join to customers before aggregation and doesn’t allow an easy “limit orders first” approach unless you restructure. A good approach is a two-step query using a CTE/subquery that selects the 50 order IDs first, then joins to items and customers: WITH recent_orders AS ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id WHERE o.created_at >= DATE '2024-01-01' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND c.country = 'US' ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 50 ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN customers c ON c.id = ro.customer_id JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = ro.id GROUP BY ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; This encourages “limit early”. In PG14, CTEs are inlined by default unless MATERIALIZED, so it should still optimize well; but if the planner tries to pull joins up, you can use MATERIALIZED to force it. Indexing changes to support that early limit: - On orders: a composite index that matches your filter + order by, ideally: CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status_created_at_id ON orders (status, created_at DESC, id, customer_id); or (created_at DESC, status, id, customer_id) depending on selectivity. Since you filter on created_at range and status IN, and order by created_at desc with limit, an index beginning with created_at DESC can be good: CREATE INDEX idx_orders_created_at_desc_status ON orders (created_at DESC, status, customer_id, id); Then the engine can do an index scan backward on created_at and apply status filter quickly. - On customers: you already have (country). But you join by id; the PK handles that. If country='US' is common (~100k of 300k), the index might not be very selective; still okay. Another option is (country, id) to support the join after filtering: CREATE INDEX idx_customers_country_id ON customers (country, id); - order_items: you have (order_id), which is exactly what you need once you restrict to 50 orders. Also ensure statistics are current: ANALYZE orders; ANALYZE customers; ANALYZE order_items; Before changing indexes, try the rewrite and see if EXPLAIN becomes nested-loop over 50 orders into order_items via idx_order_items_order_id (should be fast). If you can share the actual timing breakdown (BUFFERS and actual rows for intermediate nodes) and data distribution (how many orders since 2024-01-01 and how many paid/shipped), we can pick the best composite index order.

I tried the CTE approach (without MATERIALIZED). It got faster but still ~2.5s. EXPLAIN shows it still doing a hash join on order_items with a seq scan. It seems to ignore idx_order_items_order_id. Why would it seq-scan order_items even when recent_orders is 50 rows?

That typically happens if the planner decides it’s cheaper to scan order_items once and hash-join than to do 50 index lookups—usually due to cost parameters, correlation, or because the query got de-optimized (CTE inlined and join order changed), or because it thinks recent_orders is much larger than 50 (bad estimates). A few things to check: 1) Confirm the planner’s estimated rows for recent_orders. Run: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) WITH recent_orders AS (...) SELECT ... Look at the node for recent_orders: does it estimate rows=50, or something huge? If it estimates high, it may choose a plan that scans order_items. If estimates are wrong, update stats and consider extended stats: - ANALYZE orders; - ANALYZE customers; - CREATE STATISTICS s_orders_status_created ON orders (dependencies) FROM status, created_at; Then ANALYZE orders again. This helps with correlation between status and created_at. 2) Force materialization to prevent join reordering: WITH recent_orders AS MATERIALIZED ( ... LIMIT 50 ) This ensures the 50 rows are produced first. 3) Verify index usability: - Is order_items.order_id the same type as orders.id? (e.g., bigint vs int mismatch can prevent index usage or cause casts) - Is there an expression/cast in join? In your query it’s direct, so likely fine. - Is idx_order_items_order_id a btree? (default yes) - Is the table bloated or visibility map poor? That affects index-only scans but not index scans. 4) Consider enable_nestloop temporarily to see alternative: SET enable_hashjoin = off; SET enable_mergejoin = off; EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) ... If it becomes much faster with nested loops + index scans, you know the planner’s cost model is choosing poorly. Then you can fix via stats/indexes rather than relying on GUCs. 5) Add a covering index to reduce heap fetches when using order_items by order_id: If you frequently compute SUM(qty*unit_price), you can include qty and unit_price: CREATE INDEX idx_order_items_order_id_inc ON order_items (order_id) INCLUDE (qty, unit_price); Then for 50 orders it can do index-only scans (if visibility map permits) and reduce random I/O. But the biggest lever: force “limit first” with MATERIALIZED and have an orders index that supports picking the top 50 quickly. Can you paste the new EXPLAIN showing row estimates for recent_orders and the join to order_items?

Here’s the new plan with MATERIALIZED: WITH recent_orders AS MATERIALIZED ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id WHERE o.created_at >= DATE '2024-01-01' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND c.country = 'US' ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 50 ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN customers c ON c.id = ro.customer_id JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = ro.id GROUP BY ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS): Sort  (actual time=2500..2500 rows=50 loops=1) Sort Key: ro.created_at DESC Sort Method: quicksort  Memory: 40kB ->  HashAggregate (actual time=2400..2480 rows=50 loops=1) Group Key: ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email ->  Hash Join (actual time=80..2350 rows=600000 loops=1) Hash Cond: (oi.order_id = ro.id) ->  Seq Scan on order_items oi (actual time=0.02..2100 rows=12000000 loops=1) ->  Hash  (actual time=0.50..0.55 rows=50 loops=1) ->  CTE Scan on recent_orders ro (actual time=0.03..0.30 rows=50 loops=1) ->  Hash Join (actual time=...???)  (it joins customers too but I trimmed) So it is 50 rows in ro, but it still seq scans order_items and hashes ro. Why is it doing that? It seems crazy.

Given ro is correctly 50 rows, the only reason to still seq-scan 12M order_items is that the planner believes “scan 12M once + hash join 50 keys” is cheaper than “50 index probes into order_items”. That can happen when it expects many matches per order_id (so 50 probes would touch a large fraction of the table anyway), or when random I/O is modeled as very expensive (high random_page_cost / low effective_cache_size), or when order_items is not well clustered and it expects lots of random reads. Key diagnostic: how many order_items per order on average for those 50 orders? Your plan shows join output rows=600,000. That implies ~12,000 items per order on average for the selected orders, which is likely an estimate error or real skew. If it’s real (e.g., some huge orders), then 50 index probes could indeed retrieve hundreds of thousands of rows, potentially with lots of heap fetches. But your actual rows for the join node are 600,000, not estimated; meaning those 50 orders actually matched 600k items total. That’s 600k rows fetched—still far less than 12M, but not tiny. Still, even if you need 600k rows, doing an index scan by order_id should be better than scanning 12M, unless: - the index scan would cause 600k random heap page reads (slow on HDD / cold cache), - while seq scan is mostly sequential (fast), - and the table is large enough that scanning 12M is only ~2.1s as you see. So the planner is likely right for your I/O conditions: sequential scan is cheap. But you can often beat it by enabling index-only scans or clustering. Concrete improvements: 1) Create a covering index to avoid heap reads for those 600k rows If the query only needs qty and unit_price from order_items, do: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_oi_order_id_inc ON order_items (order_id) INCLUDE (qty, unit_price); Then VACUUM (ANALYZE) order_items; (or autovacuum) If visibility map is good, index-only scan can avoid heap, making the 600k retrieval much faster. 2) Physically cluster or partition order_items If order_items is frequently accessed by order_id, clustering helps: CLUSTER order_items USING idx_order_items_order_id; But CLUSTER is a one-time operation; you’d need maintenance. Alternatively, pg_repack can keep it clustered with less downtime. Partitioning by order_id hash or by created_at (if order_items has created_at) can reduce scanned data. But it’s heavier. 3) Adjust planner cost parameters (only if they are wrong for your hardware) Check: SHOW random_page_cost; SHOW seq_page_cost; SHOW effective_cache_size; On SSD with decent cache, random_page_cost should often be closer to 1.1–1.5 (default 4 is pessimistic). If it’s too high, PG avoids index probes. effective_cache_size should reflect OS cache + shared buffers. 4) Reduce the number of order_items rows you need to touch If you only need totals per order, consider pre-aggregating in a materialized view or storing order total in orders (denormalization) updated on write. That turns runtime aggregation into a simple lookup. But that’s an architectural change. 5) Another query shape: aggregate order_items first but only for targeted orders via semi-join Sometimes using WHERE oi.order_id IN (SELECT id FROM recent_orders) encourages index usage, but with MATERIALIZED it may still choose hash join. Example: WITH recent_orders AS MATERIALIZED (...) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, x.total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN customers c ON c.id = ro.customer_id JOIN LATERAL ( SELECT SUM(qty*unit_price) total FROM order_items oi WHERE oi.order_id = ro.id ) x ON true ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; This forces nested-loop semantics (LATERAL per order). It will do 50 index scans. Whether it’s faster depends on I/O. Given your current runtime is dominated by seq scan 2.1s, the biggest win without architectural changes is the INCLUDE index + making index-only scans possible, or clustering. Can you provide: - total size of order_items (pg_relation_size), - whether storage is SSD, - SHOW random_page_cost/effective_cache_size, - and EXPLAIN with BUFFERS for the seq scan (shared read vs hit) to see if it’s reading from disk?

random_page_cost is 4, seq_page_cost 1, effective_cache_size 4GB, shared_buffers 1GB. The server has 64GB RAM and NVMe. The BUFFERS for seq scan show shared read is huge (it’s reading a lot). order_items is ~6.5GB. Also, the join output 600k rows is real; some orders have many items. Still, scanning 12M rows seems unnecessary.

With NVMe + 64GB RAM, random_page_cost=4 and effective_cache_size=4GB are far too conservative. That will strongly bias toward seq scans and hash joins. Two actions: 1) Fix cost settings (globally in postgresql.conf if possible, or per DB): - effective_cache_size: set to something like 48GB (roughly shared_buffers + OS cache available to PG). - random_page_cost: on NVMe often 1.1–1.5. - work_mem: ensure hash aggregates don’t spill (less relevant now since you only aggregate 50 groups, but earlier it mattered). Example: ALTER SYSTEM SET effective_cache_size = '48GB'; ALTER SYSTEM SET random_page_cost = 1.2; SELECT pg_reload_conf(); Or set per session to validate: SET effective_cache_size = '48GB'; SET random_page_cost = 1.2; Then re-run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS). The planner should become more willing to do index scans on order_items. 2) Add the covering index: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_oi_order_id_inc ON order_items (order_id) INCLUDE (qty, unit_price); Then VACUUM (ANALYZE) order_items; Even with corrected costs, the planner might still choose seq scan if it believes it will touch a big fraction of the table. But given you need 600k/12M = 5% of rows, index scan is plausible. Additionally, for orders selection, you likely still benefit from a composite index on orders to get the recent 50 quickly without bitmap heap scan + sort. But your current time is dominated by order_items scan, so prioritize that. Test forcing index path: SET enable_seqscan = off; EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) ...; If it becomes faster, cost settings/indexes will get you there without disabling seqscan.

I set random_page_cost=1.2 and effective_cache_size=48GB for the session. Now the plan uses Nested Loop + Index Scan on order_items(order_id). Time dropped to ~350ms. Great. But I can’t just change server-wide settings easily; it’s a managed environment. Any query-level tricks to get the better plan without changing those settings?

Without changing GUCs, your options are limited because PostgreSQL doesn’t support optimizer hints natively. Still, you can often coax a plan: 1) Use the LATERAL pattern to force nested loops The LATERAL subquery tends to make “per-row lookup” more natural: WITH recent_orders AS MATERIALIZED ( ... ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, x.total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN customers c ON c.id = ro.customer_id JOIN LATERAL ( SELECT SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM order_items oi WHERE oi.order_id = ro.id ) x ON true ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; This makes it harder for the planner to transform it into a hash join with a full scan (though not impossible in theory, but usually it stays nested loop). 2) Reduce the attractiveness of hash join by lowering work_mem? (Not reliable) If hash join would spill, it becomes expensive, but that’s not a good strategy and can backfire. 3) Materialize recent_orders and then explicitly join with an IN list If you can accept building an array of 50 ids: WITH recent_orders AS MATERIALIZED (...) SELECT ... FROM order_items oi JOIN recent_orders ro ON ro.id = oi.order_id ... This is what you have; it chose hash join. The LATERAL is more forcing. 4) Use pg_hint_plan extension (if allowed) Some managed providers allow enabling pg_hint_plan. If you can, it’s the cleanest way to hint “NestLoop” and “IndexScan”. But often you can’t install extensions. Given you already saw huge improvement with cost settings, I’d push for adjusting them at least at the database or role level if your provider allows: ALTER ROLE app_user SET random_page_cost=1.2; ALTER ROLE app_user SET effective_cache_size='48GB'; This keeps it scoped to your application user rather than global. If even that’s not possible, LATERAL is your best query-only lever. Try it and confirm the plan.

LATERAL version works and stays fast (~400ms) even with default GUCs. Nice. Now, about the orders selection: it still does Bitmap Heap Scan on orders using idx_orders_created_at and then sorts/limits. Can we make the first step faster too?

Yes—optimize the “top-N recent qualifying orders” step. You want an index that supports: - filtering on created_at >= constant, - filtering on status IN (...), - ordering by created_at DESC, - returning id and customer_id. A good general-purpose index: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_created_status ON orders (created_at DESC, status, id) INCLUDE (customer_id); Why this order? - ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 50 benefits from leading created_at DESC: it can walk the index from newest backward and stop after 50 qualifying rows (after applying status filter). - status second allows filtering during index scan; with IN list it can still use it. - INCLUDE customer_id avoids heap fetch for customer_id if index-only scan is possible. Alternative if status is highly selective and created_at range is huge: (status, created_at DESC) might be better: CREATE INDEX ... ON orders (status, created_at DESC) INCLUDE (id, customer_id); But then ORDER BY created_at DESC with status IN can be less straightforward because status is first; it may need to scan each status separately and merge. It can still be fine with two statuses. Given you always order by created_at and limit small, (created_at DESC, status) is often best. Also consider a partial index if only those statuses matter: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_recent_paid_shipped ON orders (created_at DESC, id) INCLUDE (customer_id) WHERE status IN ('paid','shipped'); This can be much smaller and faster. Since your query always filters to those statuses, it’s ideal. Then your recent_orders CTE can become an index scan that stops quickly.
If I add the partial index on orders for paid/shipped, does it still help with created_at >= '2024-01-01'? Or would it still scan lots of index pages?

It helps because the index is ordered by created_at DESC. PostgreSQL can start from the newest entries and walk backward, applying the created_at >= cutoff as a stopping condition. It will only scan until it reaches rows older than 2024-01-01, and because you also LIMIT 50, it will likely stop long before hitting the cutoff (unless there are fewer than 50 qualifying orders after 2024-01-01 and country=US filter). The customer country filter is the tricky part: it’s on customers, not orders. So even if an order is paid/shipped and recent, it might be excluded after joining to customers. That means the index scan may need to read more than 50 orders to find 50 that belong to US customers. If US is a large fraction, it won’t need to scan much extra. If US is small, it might scan many orders. In that case, you could denormalize country onto orders, or maintain a filtered customer set and join differently, but that’s beyond indexing. Still, the partial index reduces index size (only paid/shipped) and makes the scan cheaper.
Could an index on orders(customer_id, created_at desc) help since we filter customers by country first?

Potentially, but only if the plan starts from customers filtered by country and then finds their orders. That would look like: - get US customers (100k), - for each customer, find recent paid/shipped orders, - then pick top 50 across all customers. That’s not a great shape because “top 50 across all customers” requires merging many streams; it can be done but is complex and often slower. Your current approach “start from recent orders, then filter by customer country” is usually best for top-N by time. If you frequently query “recent orders for US customers”, denormalizing country onto orders (or maintaining a materialized view of orders joined to customers) could help, but purely with indexes, the partial index on orders(created_at desc) with status filter is the best improvement.
Understood. Now about customers: currently idx_customers_country exists. Would a composite (country, id) help materially?

It can help a bit when you filter by country and then join by id because it allows an index-only retrieval of ids for that country, and can improve bitmap index scan efficiency. But in your “recent orders first” plan, customers are usually joined by PK id for just 50 rows, so customers indexing is irrelevant there. In your original plan (big hash join), customers country index mattered because it scanned 100k US customers. With the optimized “limit first” approach, you only touch 50 customers, so the PK is enough. So I wouldn’t prioritize customers indexes unless you have other queries that scan by country.
Makes sense. Another question: the GROUP BY includes c.email. If I only need email in the final output, is grouping by it necessary? Could that be slowing things?

You can avoid grouping by email by grouping only by order and then join customers after aggregation: WITH recent_orders AS MATERIALIZED ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id WHERE ... ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 50 ), totals AS ( SELECT oi.order_id, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM order_items oi JOIN recent_orders ro ON ro.id = oi.order_id GROUP BY oi.order_id ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, t.total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN totals t ON t.order_id = ro.id JOIN customers c ON c.id = ro.customer_id ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; Now the aggregation groups by order_id only, which is simpler. With LATERAL, you can also avoid grouping entirely (since each LATERAL subquery returns one row per order). For 50 orders, the difference is small, but it’s cleaner and reduces grouping keys.
I like the totals CTE. With that, and the partial index on orders, I’m down to ~200ms. Great. One last thing: any risk of CTE MATERIALIZED causing stale results or locking?

MATERIALIZED doesn’t make results stale; it just forces PostgreSQL to compute that subquery once and store it in a temporary structure for the duration of the statement. It respects MVCC like any query: you get a consistent snapshot for the statement. No special locking beyond normal SELECT behavior. The main “risk” is performance: materialization can prevent the planner from pushing filters/joins into the CTE. Here that’s exactly what you want (limit early), so it’s appropriate.
Perfect. Can you summarize the final recommended indexes and query?

Recommended indexes (in priority order): 1) On orders, partial index for the statuses you query: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_paid_shipped_recent ON orders (created_at DESC, id) INCLUDE (customer_id) WHERE status IN ('paid','shipped'); Optionally include status too if you also query other statuses, but partial makes it unnecessary. 2) On order_items, covering index to speed per-order aggregation: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_oi_order_id_inc ON order_items (order_id) INCLUDE (qty, unit_price); (You already have (order_id); this replaces/augments it. You can keep the old one, but it may be redundant.) Query (set-based totals CTE): WITH recent_orders AS MATERIALIZED ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id WHERE o.created_at >= DATE '2024-01-01' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND c.country = 'US' ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 50 ), totals AS ( SELECT oi.order_id, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM order_items oi JOIN recent_orders ro ON ro.id = oi.order_id GROUP BY oi.order_id ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, t.total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN totals t ON t.order_id = ro.id JOIN customers c ON c.id = ro.customer_id ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; If the planner still insists on scanning order_items, switch totals to a LATERAL per-order sum to force index lookups.
Thanks. While we’re here, can you explain why the original query couldn’t just use the order_items(order_id) index with the join?

In the original query, there was no early restriction to a small set of orders. You filtered orders by date and status (still ~1.5M rows) and customers by country (~100k), then joined to order_items and aggregated across the whole join. For that shape, using the order_items(order_id) index would mean probing the index for potentially hundreds of thousands of orders, which could devolve into massive random I/O. A sequential scan of order_items plus a hash join can be cheaper because it reads the table once sequentially. The key optimization was changing the query so the join to order_items happens after you’ve limited to the top 50 orders. That changes the economics from “probe index for huge number of keys” to “probe index for 50 keys”.
Got it. Could we do anything with partitioning order_items by order_id to speed queries like this?

Partitioning by order_id (hash partitioning) can help if: - you often query by order_id (or a set of order_ids), - and the partitioning allows pruning (it does for equality on partition key), - and each partition is smaller, improving cache locality and vacuum behavior. For your case—fetch items for 50 order_ids—hash partitioning could prune to 50 partitions (or fewer depending on modulus), still potentially touching many partitions. It might not be worth the complexity unless you have other maintenance benefits. Partitioning by created_at (if order_items has a timestamp) can help if you filter by time ranges. But your filter is on orders.created_at, not order_items. Given you already got to ~200ms, partitioning seems unnecessary unless you have other pressure points (vacuum bloat, retention, etc.).
Makes sense. End.

Actually not end—new issue. We have another slow query: searching orders by customer email prefix and recent date. It’s used in an admin UI with typeahead. Query: SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.status, c.email FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id WHERE c.email ILIKE 'john.%' AND o.created_at >= now() - interval '90 days' ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 20; It’s ~1.8s. We have customers(email) index (btree) and orders(created_at) index and orders(customer_id) index. How to optimize?

Two separate issues are typically at play here: 1) ILIKE + prefix and index usage - A plain btree index on customers(email) can only be used for case-sensitive prefix searches (LIKE 'john.%') in the default collation, and even then collation can matter. - ILIKE is case-insensitive; a normal btree on email usually won’t be used unless you’re using citext or a functional index on lower(email), and your pattern is compatible (prefix, not contains with leading wildcard). 2) Join order and “top-N by orders.created_at” You want the newest orders among customers whose email matches a prefix and within last 90 days. The fast path is usually: - find matching customers quickly (by email prefix), - then fetch their recent orders using an index on orders(customer_id, created_at DESC), - then merge results and take top 20. Concrete recommendations: A) Make the email predicate indexable Option 1: functional index + rewrite predicate Create: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_customers_lower_email ON customers (lower(email)); Then rewrite query: WHERE lower(c.email) LIKE lower('john.%')  -- or just 'john.%' if input normalized Better: WHERE lower(c.email) LIKE 'john.%' This enables an index range scan on idx_customers_lower_email for prefix patterns. Option 2: use citext for email If you can change column type: ALTER TABLE customers ALTER COLUMN email TYPE citext; Then you can use LIKE (case-insensitive semantics) with btree index. But changing type is heavier. Option 3: pg_trgm (best for flexible search, also works for ILIKE) If you need more than prefix (e.g., contains), enable extension and add trigram index: CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_trgm; CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_customers_email_trgm ON customers USING gin (email gin_trgm_ops); This will accelerate ILIKE 'john.%' too. For pure prefix, the lower(email) btree is smaller and often faster, but trgm is more flexible. B) Add an orders index that supports “get recent orders per customer” You already have orders(customer_id) and orders(created_at) separately. For this query, a composite index is usually the big win: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_customer_created_desc ON orders (customer_id, created_at DESC) INCLUDE (status); This allows fast retrieval of recent orders for a given customer and can be index-only for status/created_at/id (id is in the index implicitly as heap TID lookup still needed unless included; you can INCLUDE(id) too, but it’s already the PK—still, INCLUDE can help index-only scans for this specific query). C) Query shape: encourage “customers first, then orders” Once email lookup is fast, you want to join from customers to orders and keep the LIMIT applied after ordering. Try: SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.status, c.email FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id WHERE lower(c.email) LIKE 'john.%' AND o.created_at >= now() - interval '90 days' ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 20; This is logically equivalent, but can influence the planner when combined with the right indexes. If the prefix matches many customers, the planner might still choose a plan that starts from orders (last 90 days) and then filters customers. In that case, you can do a two-step approach similar to earlier: WITH matching_customers AS MATERIALIZED ( SELECT id, email FROM customers WHERE lower(email) LIKE 'john.%' ) SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.status, mc.email FROM matching_customers mc JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = mc.id WHERE o.created_at >= now() - interval '90 days' ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 20; That ensures the customer set is computed first. If it’s still too large, you can cap it (admin UI often doesn’t need to consider 100k customers for a prefix). Diagnostics to confirm: - EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) and check whether it’s doing a seq scan on customers or orders. - Check how many customers match 'john.%'. If it’s thousands, typeahead should probably require 3+ characters or cap the match set. If you paste the EXPLAIN, I can tell whether the bottleneck is the email predicate not using an index, or the join/ordering.

EXPLAIN (ANALYZE) shows it starts from orders: Bitmap Heap Scan on orders using idx_orders_created_at with condition created_at >= now()-90days, returns ~320k rows, then Hash Join to customers, then Filter on c.email ILIKE 'john.%', then Sort top-N and limit. It never uses customers(email) index.

That matches the two problems exactly: it’s treating email as a post-join filter (so it can’t use the customers index effectively), and the ILIKE prevents use of the plain btree anyway. Fixes in order of impact: 1) Make email predicate indexable If you only need prefix matching and can normalize input to lowercase, the lightest change is: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_customers_email_lower ON customers (lower(email)); Then rewrite: WHERE lower(c.email) LIKE 'john.%' This should allow an index scan on customers to find matching IDs quickly. 2) Add composite index on orders for customer_id + created_at CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_customer_created_desc ON orders (customer_id, created_at DESC) INCLUDE (status); Now, if the planner starts from customers, it can fetch recent orders per customer efficiently and then sort/limit. 3) Encourage join order Rewrite query with customers first (and optionally MATERIALIZED CTE): WITH matching_customers AS MATERIALIZED ( SELECT id, email FROM customers WHERE lower(email) LIKE 'john.%' ) SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.status, mc.email FROM matching_customers mc JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = mc.id WHERE o.created_at >= now() - interval '90 days' ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 20; Why MATERIALIZED: it prevents the planner from pulling orders up and doing “orders first” again. If you can’t add functional index but can add pg_trgm, then: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_customers_email_trgm ON customers USING gin (email gin_trgm_ops); and keep ILIKE. This tends to work well and is robust to case and non-prefix patterns, at the expense of a larger index and slower writes. Given your current plan scans 320k orders, a “customers first” plan will likely be far faster if the prefix is selective (and most prefixes are).
Prefix can be short, like “jo”. That might match lots of customers. Still want it fast. Any strategies besides “require 3 chars”?

If “jo” matches a large fraction, the query is inherently less selective, so you’re fighting physics. But you can still make it tolerable: 1) Use trigram index on email For short prefixes, the lower(email) btree still works (it’s a range scan), but it may return many rows quickly; then the expensive part becomes pulling orders for too many customers. Trigram doesn’t fix that; it just finds customers fast. 2) Switch strategy: start from recent orders, but reduce the cost of checking email Your current plan starts from recent orders (320k) and then hashes customers and checks ILIKE on each joined row. If you make customer email check cheap and “early”, that might be acceptable. Two improvements for that path: - Ensure join to customers is index lookup rather than hash join with a big hash table, by making orders path produce fewer rows or enabling a plan that does nested loop lookups. But hints aren’t available. - Denormalize a searchable email key onto orders (e.g., customer_email_lower) and index it alongside created_at. This is the “fastest UI” solution but requires data redundancy and update logic when a customer changes email. 3) A middle ground: precompute matching customer IDs and cap them For typeahead, you can: - Find top N customers matching prefix (say 500) ordered by something (recent activity, customer id, email), then fetch orders for those customers. This gives predictable worst-case. Example: WITH matching_customers AS MATERIALIZED ( SELECT id, email FROM customers WHERE lower(email) LIKE 'jo.%' ORDER BY email LIMIT 500 ) SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.status, mc.email FROM matching_customers mc JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = mc.id WHERE o.created_at >= now() - interval '90 days' ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 20; This changes semantics slightly (you might miss an order for a customer whose email matches but is outside the first 500 by email). For admin typeahead, that’s often acceptable; if not, you can order matching_customers by “most recent order date” but that becomes recursive. 4) Use a separate “search” table keyed for UI Common pattern: maintain a table like customer_search (customer_id, email_lower, last_order_at) updated asynchronously. Then you can: - search customers by prefix quickly, - order customers by last_order_at to prioritize those with recent orders, - then fetch orders for the top few customers. If you need correctness and speed for very unselective prefixes, you usually need either: - a constraint on input length, or - a ranked/capped search UX, or - denormalized/search-optimized structures. If you want to keep exact semantics, I’d do: - idx_customers_lower_email - idx_orders_customer_created_desc and accept that “jo” might still be slower because it legitimately matches a lot.
If we denormalize customer_email_lower onto orders, what indexes would you suggest and how to keep it consistent?

Schema change: - Add column: orders.customer_email_lower text - Populate it from customers.email (lowercased) - Keep it in sync Indexes: For your query pattern (email prefix + recent orders + order by created_at desc limit): A partial index can be very effective if you always filter to recent window, but since “now()-90 days” moves, partial index on a rolling window is awkward. Instead: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_email_created_desc ON orders (customer_email_lower, created_at DESC) INCLUDE (status); Then query: WHERE customer_email_lower LIKE 'john.%' AND created_at >= now() - interval '90 days' ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20; This becomes a simple index range scan on email prefix, already ordered by created_at desc within each email value; but note: ordering by created_at desc across all emails matching the prefix isn’t perfectly aligned with (email, created_at) ordering. The index will group by email first, then created_at within each email. For global “most recent across all matching emails”, you’d prefer (created_at DESC, customer_email_lower) but then prefix search becomes harder. So you have to choose: - If you want perfect ORDER BY created_at DESC with LIMIT, lead with created_at: CREATE INDEX ON orders (created_at DESC) INCLUDE (customer_email_lower, status); But then email prefix is just a filter during scan, could still be okay if 90 days is not huge. - If you want perfect prefix search, lead with customer_email_lower: CREATE INDEX ON orders (customer_email_lower, created_at DESC) ... Then you may need to scan multiple email groups to find the top 20 by created_at; might still be fine if prefix is selective. A compromise is two indexes and let planner choose, but that’s extra write cost. Consistency: - Trigger on orders insert/update to set customer_email_lower from customers at insert time. - Trigger on customers email update to update all related orders (potentially many rows—can be expensive). Because customer email updates are usually rare, you can do it asynchronously: - Store customer_email_lower at insert time in orders. - On customer email change, enqueue a job to update orders where customer_id=... in batches. If you need strict real-time consistency, you pay the update cost. If “eventual consistency” is acceptable for admin search, async is better. Before denormalizing, I’d try the normalized approach with functional index + composite orders(customer_id, created_at desc) and see if it meets needs.
Ok. Switching back: for the normalized approach, would an index on orders(created_at desc, customer_id) help for the admin query if planner insists on starting from orders?

Yes, if the plan starts from “recent orders” and then joins to customers, an index on orders(created_at DESC, customer_id) can help in two ways: - It can produce rows already in the desired order (created_at desc), enabling an index scan that stops early due to LIMIT (top-N). - It can carry customer_id right in the index, reducing heap visits (especially if you INCLUDE status/id). But the email filter is on customers, so stopping early is only possible if a reasonable fraction of recent orders belong to customers whose email matches. For short prefixes, that might be true; for long prefixes, you might stop very early. Index suggestion for that “orders-first” strategy: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_created_desc_customer ON orders (created_at DESC, customer_id) INCLUDE (status, id); Then query can do an index scan on orders from newest backwards, join to customers by PK for each row, check email predicate, and stop when it finds 20 matches. That would likely be a Nested Loop plan (orders index scan -> customers lookup). PostgreSQL might still choose a hash join if it expects many rows, but the LIMIT + order can make nested loop attractive. To make customers email check indexable in that nested loop, you still need to avoid ILIKE without an indexable form. But note: in a nested loop with only 20–200 customer lookups, even a non-indexable ILIKE check is cheap because it’s just evaluating a string, not scanning customers. So if you can get the plan to: - walk recent orders in created_at desc order - join each order to its customer - test email prefix - stop at 20 then you might not need to index email at all for this specific query. This is actually a very good strategy for typeahead when you care about “recent orders” more than “all customers”. You can encourage it by rewriting: SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.status, c.email FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id WHERE o.created_at >= now() - interval '90 days' AND c.email ILIKE 'john.%' ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 20; Same as yours, but with the created_at predicate first doesn’t matter semantically; planner might still do orders-first. If you add idx_orders_created_desc_customer and keep random_page_cost conservative, it may still choose bitmap heap scan though. You can sometimes make it prefer the ordered index scan by also dropping/avoiding the plain idx_orders_created_at, but that’s risky for other queries. Best general approach remains: make email predicate indexable + composite index orders(customer_id, created_at desc). But if you want “stop early by created_at”, then idx_orders_created_desc_customer is a strong addition.
We can afford one new index on orders, not two. Which one gives more overall benefit across typical workload: (customer_id, created_at desc) or (created_at desc, customer_id)?

If your workload has many queries of the form: - “for a given customer (or small set of customers), show their recent orders” then (customer_id, created_at DESC) is the clear winner. It’s the canonical index for per-customer history pages, billing screens, etc. If your workload has many queries of the form: - “show the most recent orders overall, optionally with some filters, LIMIT N” then (created_at DESC, customer_id) tends to be better. For the specific admin typeahead query, the better one depends on which side is more selective: - If email prefix narrows to a few customers, (customer_id, created_at DESC) is best. - If prefix is broad and you mostly care about “recent orders”, then (created_at DESC, customer_id) can be best because it can stop early by LIMIT. Given you mentioned typeahead prefixes can be very short (“jo”), I’d lean slightly toward (created_at DESC, customer_id) for that UI query, because it can find 20 matches without scanning all matching customers’ orders, as long as matches occur reasonably in recent orders. But for overall system design, (customer_id, created_at DESC) is more broadly useful and usually more selective. If you already have orders(customer_id) and orders(created_at) separate, adding (customer_id, created_at DESC) often replaces both for many queries. If you can share 2–3 other common orders queries (by customer? by date? by status?), I can pick the better “one index” choice with more confidence.

Common queries: 1) Customer profile page: last 50 orders for customer_id. 2) Ops dashboard: last 1000 paid/shipped orders across all customers, order by created_at desc. 3) Finance: orders in date range filtered by status, grouped by day. Given that, pick one index.

Pick: (customer_id, created_at DESC) with INCLUDE(status), and keep your existing created_at index for the dashboard/date-range queries if you can. But since you said only one new index, the new composite should target the biggest pain and replace at least one existing index. Proposed: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_customer_created_desc ON orders (customer_id, created_at DESC) INCLUDE (status); Impact: 1) Customer profile (last 50 by customer): huge win; becomes an index scan that stops at 50. 2) Ops dashboard (recent paid/shipped across all customers): this index won’t help much because customer_id is first. For that, your earlier partial index on (created_at DESC) WHERE status IN (...) is ideal, but that would be a second index. 3) Finance date range + status grouped by day: your existing idx_orders_created_at likely continues to be used; a BRIN on created_at can also be great for large append-only tables, but that’s another index type. If you must choose exactly one new index and you’re allowed to drop an old one, you could: - Create the partial paid/shipped recent index (created_at desc) WHERE status IN (...) and drop the plain created_at index, because the partial covers a lot of dashboard traffic while being smaller. But it would not help finance queries for other statuses, and it wouldn’t help customer profile as much as (customer_id, created_at). So the safest “overall workload” choice is: - add (customer_id, created_at DESC) INCLUDE(status) and keep current created_at index for dashboard/finance until you can revisit index budget.

That’s helpful. Another performance topic: we suspect table bloat on order_items and orders. Autovacuum runs but performance degrades over time. How do we measure bloat and decide between VACUUM FULL, CLUSTER, or pg_repack?

Measuring bloat and choosing the remediation depends on whether you’re suffering from: - dead tuples taking space (bloat), - poor correlation/clustering (random I/O), - or index bloat specifically.
