How to measure: 1) Basic visibility: dead tuples and vacuum stats Check per table: SELECT relname, n_live_tup, n_dead_tup, last_vacuum, last_autovacuum, last_analyze, last_autoanalyze, vacuum_count, autovacuum_count FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE relname IN ('orders','order_items'); If n_dead_tup is large relative to n_live_tup (say >10–20% consistently), you likely have bloat or vacuum lag. 2) Table and index sizes SELECT relname, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(relid)) AS table_size, pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(relid) - pg_relation_size(relid)) AS indexes_size, pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(relid)) AS total_size FROM pg_catalog.pg_statio_user_tables WHERE relname IN ('orders','order_items'); And per index: SELECT indexrelname, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS index_size, idx_scan FROM pg_stat_user_indexes WHERE relname IN ('orders','order_items') ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC; 3) Bloat estimation The most common approach uses the pgstattuple extension (if allowed): CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pgstattuple; SELECT * FROM pgstattuple('public.order_items'); This reports approx free space, dead tuple percentage. If you can’t use extensions, there are SQL bloat estimate queries (less accurate) based on pg_class/pg_stats; but pgstattuple is best. Deciding remediation: A) VACUUM (regular) / autovacuum tuning - Regular VACUUM doesn’t shrink files; it just marks dead tuples reusable. - If bloat is from not vacuuming enough, tune autovacuum thresholds per table (especially order_items if high churn). Example per table: ALTER TABLE order_items SET ( autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.02, autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor = 0.01, autovacuum_vacuum_threshold = 5000, autovacuum_analyze_threshold = 5000 ); And ensure autovacuum_max_workers / maintenance_work_mem are adequate (managed env may limit). B) VACUUM FULL - Rewrites the table and compacts it, but requires an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock for the duration (blocks reads/writes). Usually unacceptable for big hot tables. C) CLUSTER - Rewrites table ordered by an index (improves locality for that access pattern) and also compacts. - Also requires strong locks and is one-time; table will gradually drift as writes happen. D) pg_repack (often best) - Rebuilds table and indexes with minimal locking (brief lock at the end). - Compacts bloat like VACUUM FULL and can also order by an index (depending on options). - Requires installing extension/binary; in managed environments, sometimes available, sometimes not. Rule of thumb: - If you can install/use pg_repack: use it periodically for large, heavily updated tables. - If you can tolerate downtime/locking: VACUUM FULL or CLUSTER can work, but plan maintenance window. - If bloat is mild: tune autovacuum and rely on regular VACUUM, plus occasional REINDEX for bloated indexes. Also: index bloat can be addressed with REINDEX CONCURRENTLY: REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_order_items_order_id; This is less disruptive and sometimes enough if table bloat isn’t the main issue. If you tell me: - update/delete rates on orders/order_items, - whether order_items is append-only or frequently updated, - and whether pg_repack/pgstattuple extensions are allowed, I can suggest a concrete maintenance plan and autovacuum settings.

order_items is mostly insert-only but we do occasional updates (qty adjustments) and deletes (fraud). orders gets status updates and occasional deletes. We can’t install arbitrary extensions; pgstattuple not available. We can run REINDEX CONCURRENTLY though. How do we detect bloat without extensions, and what maintenance plan fits?

Without pgstattuple, you can still get useful signals, just not an exact “bloat %”. Detecting likely bloat: 1) Dead tuples and vacuum lag (strong indicator) SELECT relname, n_live_tup, n_dead_tup, round(100.0*n_dead_tup/nullif(n_live_tup,0),2) AS dead_pct, last_autovacuum, last_autoanalyze FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE relname IN ('orders','order_items'); If dead_pct stays high and last_autovacuum is old or autovacuum can’t keep up, you’ll see it. 2) Table growth without matching live tuples (weak but useful) Track over time (daily): - pg_total_relation_size('order_items') - n_live_tup If size grows while n_live_tup is flat, bloat is likely. 3) Correlation / clustering quality For indexes where locality matters (order_items by order_id), check correlation: SELECT attname, correlation FROM pg_stats WHERE schemaname='public' AND tablename='order_items' AND attname='order_id'; Correlation near 1 or -1 means well-ordered; near 0 means scattered. Scattered data makes index scans more random-I/O heavy and can “feel like bloat”. 4) Index usage and size vs table size If an index is huge compared to table and has low idx_scan, it might be bloated/unnecessary. From pg_stat_user_indexes + pg_relation_size. Maintenance plan given constraints: A) Make autovacuum more aggressive on orders (status updates create dead tuples) Orders has frequent UPDATEs; autovacuum needs to run often enough to prevent dead tuple buildup and also freeze. Per-table autovacuum settings are usually allowed even in managed env: ALTER TABLE orders SET ( autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.02, autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor = 0.01, autovacuum_vacuum_threshold = 5000, autovacuum_analyze_threshold = 5000 ); If orders is 2M rows, default vacuum_scale_factor 0.2 means 400k row changes before vacuum triggers—too high for churny tables. B) order_items: if mostly insert-only, bloat should be lower, but deletes/updates still matter Set slightly less aggressive than orders, unless you delete a lot: ALTER TABLE order_items SET ( autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.05, autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor = 0.02, autovacuum_vacuum_threshold = 10000, autovacuum_analyze_threshold = 10000 ); C) Periodic REINDEX CONCURRENTLY on high-churn indexes Indexes can bloat even when table is okay, especially on columns updated often (orders.status if indexed, created_at if inserts are random, etc.). Monthly or as-needed: REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_created_at; REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_customer_id; REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_order_items_order_id; Better: identify which indexes are growing fastest and reindex those. D) Consider fillfactor on orders (reduces page splits, can reduce bloat from updates) If orders rows are updated multiple times (status), set lower fillfactor so updates fit on same page (HOT updates), reducing index churn and bloat: ALTER TABLE orders SET (fillfactor = 80); Then you need a rewrite to apply fully (e.g., during a maintenance rebuild), but it can still help going forward for new pages. Also consider lowering fillfactor on indexes that suffer splits. E) Monitor autovacuum effectiveness Look at: SELECT relname, vacuum_count, autovacuum_count, analyze_count, autoanalyze_count FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE relname IN ('orders','order_items'); And check if autovacuum is getting cancelled or falling behind (managed logs may show). If you share the current n_dead_tup and last_autovacuum for both tables, plus how many updates/day on orders, I can suggest tighter/looser thresholds and whether REINDEX cadence should be weekly vs monthly.

n_dead_tup on orders hovers around 250k, n_live_tup 2M, last_autovacuum is within 1 day. order_items n_dead_tup 50k, n_live_tup 12M. Yet performance still degrades. Could it be cache or index correlation instead of bloat?

Yes—those dead tuple ratios (orders ~12.5%, order_items ~0.4%) aren’t outrageous, and autovacuum running daily suggests it’s not completely stuck. Performance degradation over time can be from: 1) Index/table correlation degrading (data becoming less physically ordered) Even with low dead tuples, if order_items becomes increasingly scattered on disk relative to order_id, index scans become more random and slower. Check correlation as mentioned: SELECT correlation FROM pg_stats WHERE schemaname='public' AND tablename='order_items' AND attname='order_id'; Also check orders correlation on created_at: SELECT correlation FROM pg_stats WHERE schemaname='public' AND tablename='orders' AND attname='created_at'; If created_at correlation drops, “recent orders” queries can become less sequential. 2) Cache churn / working set changes If your workload grows, the working set might exceed RAM, leading to more reads. Your earlier BUFFERS showed big shared reads; that can vary with cache warmth. Track: - pg_stat_database blks_read vs blks_hit over time - OS-level cache and I/O (if you have metrics) 3) Index bloat or fragmentation despite low dead tuples Index bloat can happen even if table dead tuples are moderate, especially with UPDATEs on indexed columns, or with random insert patterns. You can infer index bloat by: - index size growth over time - REINDEX temporarily improving performance (a strong signal) 4) Statistics drift / bad plans If data distribution changes (more recent orders, more US customers, etc.) and analyze is infrequent, plans can regress. But you said autovacuum runs; still check last_autoanalyze and consider lowering analyze thresholds on orders. Given your symptoms, a pragmatic test: - Pick the specific query that degrades. - Run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) when it’s “slow”. - REINDEX CONCURRENTLY the key index involved (e.g., idx_order_items_order_id or orders created_at index). - Re-run the query. If it improves materially, index bloat/fragmentation is likely. Also: if correlation is low and you rely on index scans by order_id, CLUSTER would help but is disruptive. Without that option, you can approximate by: - periodically REINDEX (helps index, not heap ordering) - consider partitioning future data (heavy) - or accept seq scans where appropriate. If you tell me which exact query regresses and which index it relies on, we can decide whether correlation or index bloat is the likely culprit and what maintenance action gives the best ROI in your constraints.

The query that regresses is the “recent orders totals” one we optimized earlier. It’s fast after a deploy (warm cache?) but drifts from 200ms to ~900ms after a few days. EXPLAIN still shows the same plan (LATERAL index scans into order_items). Buffers show more reads over time.

If the plan stays stable and only buffers shift from hits to reads, it’s almost certainly cache/IO pressure rather than planner/bloat. What’s happening: - The LATERAL plan does many index probes into order_items for 50 orders. - If the relevant index and heap pages are in cache, it’s very fast. - As cache churns (other workloads, larger working set), those pages get evicted, so each probe causes disk reads, and latency rises. Ways to make it less cache-sensitive: 1) Make it more index-only Your INCLUDE index on order_items(order_id) INCLUDE(qty, unit_price) helps only if index-only scan can be used and visibility map is set (pages all-visible). If you still see heap fetches, it will read heap pages too, doubling IO. Check in EXPLAIN whether it says “Index Only Scan” and “Heap Fetches:”. If it’s Index Scan (not only), consider: - Ensure the INCLUDE index exists and is being used. - Improve vacuum so visibility map stays current (autovacuum needs to mark pages all-visible; frequent updates prevent that). - If order_items has occasional updates/deletes, those pages won’t be all-visible, reducing index-only effectiveness. 2) Reduce random IO by batching / set-based aggregation Counterintuitive, but if cache is cold, the hash join + seq scan might be faster and more stable because it’s mostly sequential IO. Your earlier 2.1s seq scan was “stable”. You can choose between: - LATERAL (fast when cached, volatile when cold) - seq scan (slower but stable) If you want predictable latency, you might prefer a set-based aggregation that can use bitmap scans rather than 50 separate probes. Example: WITH recent_orders AS MATERIALIZED (... LIMIT 50) SELECT ... FROM order_items oi JOIN recent_orders ro ON ro.id = oi.order_id GROUP BY ... This might choose a bitmap index scan on order_items(order_id) with a BitmapOr/BitmapAnd strategy, which can be more IO-efficient than 50 independent index scans. 3) Precompute totals If you store order_total on orders and maintain it transactionally (or via triggers), the query becomes a simple lookup of 50 rows—much less IO and highly cache-friendly. 4) Increase cache residency / reduce working set Not always under DB control in managed env, but you can: - ensure effective_cache_size reflects reality (helps planner choose better plans; doesn’t change cache itself) - reduce other large scans (e.g., avoid accidental seq scans) - add more RAM / tune shared_buffers if allowed (managed often fixed) 5) Use prepared statements carefully If you use prepared statements with generic plans, sometimes the planner chooses a plan that isn’t ideal for specific parameter values. But you said plan is stable and good, so less likely. Next step: check if your LATERAL subquery is doing Index Scan vs Index Only Scan and how many heap fetches it has when slow vs fast. If heap fetches are high, we can focus on improving index-only viability (vacuum, fillfactor, minimizing updates on order_items rows). If it’s already index-only but still reading index pages from disk, then it’s pure cache churn and precomputing totals is the most robust fix.

In the slow case, it is Index Only Scan on idx_oi_order_id_inc, but Heap Fetches is still like 80k. In the fast case, heap fetches are similar but hits dominate. Why heap fetches at all if it’s index-only?

“Index Only Scan” means PostgreSQL will try to satisfy the query from the index, but it can only skip the heap if the visibility map says the heap page is all-visible for your snapshot. If not all-visible, it must visit the heap to check tuple visibility—those are counted as Heap Fetches. So index-only scan can still do many heap fetches when: - the table has recent inserts/updates and pages haven’t been vacuumed to set all-visible, - the table has any updated/deleted tuples on those pages, - autovacuum hasn’t caught up, or vacuum can’t mark all-visible due to ongoing churn, - long-running transactions prevent vacuum from advancing visibility. What to do: 1) Check for long-running transactions Long transactions prevent vacuum from marking tuples all-visible. Look for old xmin: SELECT pid, usename, state, xact_start, query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE xact_start IS NOT NULL ORDER BY xact_start ASC LIMIT 20; If you see transactions running for hours/days, fix that (app side). This is a very common cause. 2) Make autovacuum more aggressive on order_items, even if dead tuples are low Marking pages all-visible requires vacuum, not just analyze. Insert-only tables still need vacuum to set visibility map bits. Try: ALTER TABLE order_items SET ( autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.01, autovacuum_vacuum_threshold = 20000 ); The threshold should reflect insert rate; scale_factor triggers based on changed tuples since last vacuum. For append-heavy tables, you want vacuum often enough to mark older pages all-visible. 3) Consider manual VACUUM (not FULL) during low traffic VACUUM order_items; will help set visibility map bits without exclusive locks. It still takes resources but is online. 4) Reduce updates on order_items rows if possible If qty adjustments are common, you lose all-visible on hot pages. Consider storing adjustments separately (audit/adjustment table) and computing effective qty, or apply adjustments only before “finalization”. Might be too big a change, but it’s the root cause. 5) Check HOT update ratio If updates avoid touching indexed columns and there’s room on page (fillfactor), they can be HOT updates and reduce index churn—but visibility still affected. Still, setting a lower fillfactor on order_items can reduce page splits and may help vacuum efficiency: ALTER TABLE order_items SET (fillfactor = 90); (Requires rewrite to fully apply, but helps future.) Since heap fetches are 80k for just 50 orders, that suggests those 600k index entries span many heap pages that aren’t all-visible. Improving vacuum/visibility map and eliminating long transactions is the best way to make index-only truly “only”.
We do have a logical replication slot and a CDC consumer that sometimes falls behind. Could that prevent vacuum from marking all-visible?

Replication slots can prevent removal of old WAL, but they do not directly prevent vacuum from marking pages all-visible. What can prevent vacuum progress is: - long-running transactions (including idle-in-transaction sessions), - old snapshots held by replication or logical decoding in a way that affects xmin horizon. Logical decoding can indeed hold back vacuum’s ability to remove dead tuples because it may need to keep tuples for decoding; it influences the “oldest xmin” horizon (depends on setup/version and whether the decoding plugin holds snapshots). In practice, a lagging logical replication slot can contribute to vacuum not being able to clean up dead tuples, and can keep tuples “recent” from vacuum’s perspective. Even if dead tuples aren’t huge, it can still impact visibility map advancement because visibility map requires that all tuples on a page are visible to all transactions. If xmin horizon is held back, fewer pages qualify as all-visible. What to check: 1) Replication slot lag and xmin impact SELECT slot_name, plugin, slot_type, active, pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn)) AS restart_lsn_lag, pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), confirmed_flush_lsn)) AS flush_lsn_lag, xmin FROM pg_replication_slots; If xmin is set and very old, it can hold back vacuum. 2) Oldest xmin in the system You can’t directly “see” global xmin easily, but symptoms show up as: - vacuum not removing dead tuples - pg_stat_activity showing old xact_start - replication slot xmin being old Mitigations: - Ensure the CDC consumer keeps up; avoid letting slots lag for long. - If you can, use “temporary” slots or drop/recreate when consumer is down (tradeoff: data loss for CDC). - For some managed systems, there are best practices to avoid logical decoding holding back vacuum (e.g., consume continuously, avoid huge transactions, avoid decoding from very old LSN). If your slot is the culprit, you’ll often see dead tuples accumulating and vacuum “unable to remove” (in logs). But your dead tuple count isn’t huge; still, it could be enough to prevent many pages from becoming all-visible, hurting index-only scans. If you paste the pg_replication_slots output (mask slot names if needed) and whether xmin is populated, I can interpret whether it’s likely affecting you.

pg_replication_slots shows one logical slot with xmin set and it’s about 2 days old when consumer lags. restart_lsn_lag can be 30GB. So likely yes. If we can’t fix consumer immediately, any query-level workaround to reduce dependency on visibility map?

If heap fetches are coming from visibility checks, and you can’t get pages all-visible due to xmin horizon being held back, you can’t “force” index-only scans to avoid heap fetches—they’ll still need to confirm visibility. Workarounds are about changing the IO pattern so it’s less random and less sensitive to heap lookups: 1) Prefer a plan that does fewer random heap visits Sometimes switching from “50 separate index-only scans” to a bitmap heap scan can be better because it batches heap page visits. Try rewriting totals as a set-based aggregation (not LATERAL): WITH recent_orders AS MATERIALIZED (... LIMIT 50) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = ro.id JOIN customers c ON c.id = ro.customer_id GROUP BY ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; Then see if planner chooses Bitmap Index Scan on order_items(order_id) + Bitmap Heap Scan. That tends to touch each heap page once, which can outperform many point lookups when heap fetches are inevitable. 2) Pre-aggregate order_items into a summary table This avoids touching order_items at read time. Even if the CDC slot delays vacuum, reads won’t suffer. 3) If you keep LATERAL, reduce heap fetches by reducing touched tuples Not much you can do, but you can sometimes reduce number of order_items rows by filtering (e.g., exclude canceled items) if applicable. 4) Operational: run VACUUM more frequently won’t fully help if xmin horizon is held back, but it can still help with pruning and visibility where possible. If you need a “query-level knob” only, the bitmap approach is the best candidate. It may be slightly slower when everything is cached, but more stable under visibility-map constraints and disk reads. Given the CDC slot situation, the real fix is to prevent xmin from being held back (keep consumer caught up, or redesign CDC). Otherwise index-only scans won’t become truly heap-free, and you’ll continue seeing random reads over time.
For the bitmap approach, how can I encourage PostgreSQL to choose Bitmap Heap Scan on order_items instead of nested loop index scans?

Without hints, you mostly influence it via query shape and row estimates. Practical levers: 1) Use set-based join + GROUP BY (avoid LATERAL) The LATERAL form strongly encourages nested loops. The plain join/aggregate form makes bitmap/hash more likely, especially if it expects many matching rows (which you do: ~600k). 2) Ensure recent_orders is MATERIALIZED This gives the planner a concrete small relation of 50 rows and may choose a BitmapOr over those 50 order_ids. 3) Use an explicit IN (SELECT ...) semi-join Sometimes this pattern leads to bitmap scans: WITH recent_orders AS MATERIALIZED (...) SELECT oi.order_id, SUM(oi.qty*oi.unit_price) FROM order_items oi WHERE oi.order_id IN (SELECT id FROM recent_orders) GROUP BY oi.order_id; Then join that result back to recent_orders/customers. The IN subquery can be planned as a hashed subplan and combined with bitmap scans. 4) Increase work_mem (session-level if allowed) so bitmap stays in memory If bitmap spills, it may choose a different plan. But you said managed env; you might still be able to SET work_mem for the session/transaction. Example test: SET work_mem = '128MB'; EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) ... 5) Better statistics on order_items(order_id) distribution If PG underestimates matches, it may prefer nested loop. Make sure order_items is analyzed and consider raising statistics target on order_id if distribution is skewed: ALTER TABLE order_items ALTER COLUMN order_id SET STATISTICS 1000; ANALYZE order_items; This helps it estimate “600k rows for 50 order_ids” more accurately, making bitmap more attractive. You can’t guarantee the bitmap plan, but removing LATERAL and improving stats usually gets you there.
Great. I think we have a path: fix CDC lag, adjust stats, and choose query shape depending on stability. That’s all.
I’m designing a REST API for a personal project and want feedback on endpoint structure. Is it better to use /users/{id}/posts or /posts?user_id={id} for fetching a user's posts?

Both are acceptable patterns; which is “better” depends on what you want the URL to communicate and how you expect clients to query. - /users/{id}/posts expresses a clear containment relationship: posts belonging to a specific user. It’s great when “posts of a user” is a first-class concept and you often navigate from user → posts. - /posts?user_id={id} treats posts as a top-level collection and uses filtering. It’s great when posts are often queried by many dimensions (user, tag, date range, status) and you want a consistent pattern for search/filter. Common approach: expose both, but choose one as canonical. If you do, make sure the response semantics (pagination, sorting, fields) are identical, and consider returning a 301/308 or a link relation to guide clients. If you want a single recommendation: prefer /posts?user_id=… if you foresee many filters and want uniformity; prefer /users/{id}/posts if the API is more navigational and resource-oriented.
I’m leaning toward uniform filtering, because I’ll have other filters like tag, since, until, and maybe visibility. But I also like the readability of nested routes. Is it weird to combine them?

It’s not weird, but it can create subtle duplication and inconsistencies if you’re not disciplined. Combining is fine if: 1) You define one canonical representation of “a page of posts” (same default sort, same pagination format, same fields, same error behavior). 2) You ensure nested routes are strictly convenience aliases, not separate resources with different rules. 3) You avoid deep nesting beyond one level (e.g., /users/{id}/posts/{postId}/comments becomes hard to maintain and can be misleading if comments aren’t strictly contained). A pattern that often works well: - Keep top-level collection endpoints for query: GET /posts?user_id=…&tag=…&since=… - Keep nested endpoints for “create under parent”: POST /users/{id}/posts (optional) or POST /posts with user_id in body (pick one; I’d pick POST /posts with author_id field). - Keep single-resource endpoints always top-level: GET /posts/{postId}, PATCH /posts/{postId}, DELETE /posts/{postId} So, you can keep readability for a couple of common navigations without letting nested paths proliferate.
On the topic of creating posts, is it more RESTful to do POST /users/{id}/posts or POST /posts with {user_id: ...} in the body?

Both are “RESTful enough,” but they emphasize different constraints. POST /users/{id}/posts: - Pros: user is part of the URL, so it’s hard to create a post for the wrong user; it reads like “create a post under this user.” - Cons: if later you allow posts to have multiple authors, or you change the model (e.g., organizations), the URL bakes in a relationship that might evolve. POST /posts with {author_id: …} (or infer author from auth): - Pros: posts are first-class; URL stays stable even if your domain changes; you can support different creation contexts without new endpoints. - Cons: requires a body field or auth context; you must validate author_id and permissions. If your API will have authentication, a common design is: - POST /posts with body containing content and maybe metadata, and the server infers author from the access token. That avoids user_id entirely for normal clients. - Admin-like flows can still specify author_id if needed, but that’s optional and permissioned. If you don’t have auth in scope, POST /posts with author_id is simpler and consistent with query filtering.
That makes sense. I do want to add auth later, and I like the idea that the server infers author. For now, I’ll accept author_id in the body but maybe mark it as “ignored when authenticated.”

That’s a reasonable transition plan. If you do it, document the rule clearly: - If authenticated: author_id in request is either ignored or must match the authenticated user (pick one). I prefer “must match” because it catches client bugs early. - If unauthenticated (dev mode): author_id required. Also consider versioning implications: you can keep a stable endpoint while evolving validation behavior. Just be explicit in docs and error responses.
Speaking of versioning, should I do /v1/posts or use Accept headers like application/vnd.myapi.v1+json?

For most APIs, /v1 in the path is the pragmatic choice: - Easier to test in a browser/curl - Easier for proxies, caches, and logs - Clear in documentation and client code Header-based versioning is more “pure” in some interpretations (resource URL stays stable), but it tends to complicate tooling and debugging. It also doesn’t eliminate breaking changes—you still have to coordinate clients. A good compromise: - Use /v1 in the path for major versioning. - Use media type or Accept headers for representation negotiation (e.g., JSON vs. something else), not for versioning. And ideally: minimize breaking changes by designing extensible responses (additive fields, stable IDs, consistent error formats).
Okay. Next: pagination. For GET /posts, should I use limit/offset or cursor-based pagination?

Cursor-based pagination is generally better for feeds and changing datasets because it avoids duplicates/skips when new items are inserted or removed. - limit/offset: - Simple to understand - Good for small datasets or admin UIs - Can be slow for large offsets - Inconsistent when data changes between requests - cursor: - Stable “next page” traversal - More efficient with proper indexing - Slightly more complex for clients If posts are time-ordered and you expect “infinite scroll,” cursor is a strong default: - GET /posts?limit=20&cursor=abc123 Response includes: - items - next_cursor (or next link) - maybe prev_cursor if you want backward navigation Implementation detail: cursor can be an encoded tuple like (created_at, id) to guarantee deterministic order. Use a stable sort: created_at desc, id desc.
I’ve seen APIs return “next” and “prev” URLs in a links object. Is that better than returning cursors?

Returning full URLs (HATEOAS-ish) is often more ergonomic: - Clients can follow the link without constructing query strings. - You can change pagination mechanics without changing client logic as much. A common response shape: { "data": [ ... ], "links": { "self": "/v1/posts?limit=20", "next": "/v1/posts?limit=20&cursor=...", "prev": null }, "meta": { "limit": 20 } } You can still include the raw cursor in meta if you want. But links are a nice touch. If you do links, make sure they’re absolute or clearly documented as relative. Also be consistent across all list endpoints.

For filtering, I’m considering a lot of query params: user_id, tag, q (search), since, until, sort, order, visibility, etc. At some point does it become too messy? Would you ever use POST /posts/search with a JSON body?

It can get messy, and POST-based search can be justified, but there are tradeoffs. GET with query params: - Pros: cacheable, shareable URLs, easy to debug - Cons: URL length limits, complex nested filters are awkward, arrays and boolean logic can be ugly POST /posts/search with JSON body: - Pros: expressive filters (AND/OR groups), long queries, structured sort specs - Cons: not cache-friendly by default, less “REST-y” to some, harder to link/share A practical approach: - Keep simple filters on GET /posts with query params for common use cases. - Introduce POST /posts/search only if you truly need complex boolean logic or large filter payloads. If you do POST search, define a stable schema: { "filter": { "author_id": "...", "tags": ["a","b"], "created_at": {"gte":"...","lt":"..."}, "visibility": ["public"] }, "sort": [{"field":"created_at","direction":"desc"}], "page": {"limit": 50, "cursor": "..."} } But don’t do it prematurely. Many APIs live happily with query params plus some conventions for arrays and ranges.
Arrays in query strings always confuse me. What convention do you recommend? tags=foo&tags=bar or tags=foo,bar or tags[]=foo&tags[]=bar?

Pick one and be consistent across the API. My preference: repeated keys (tags=foo&tags=bar) because it’s widely supported and unambiguous. - Repeated keys: - tags=foo&tags=bar - Works well with many frameworks - Natural mapping to arrays - Comma-separated: - tags=foo,bar - Compact, but requires escaping commas inside values and careful parsing - Bracket notation: - tags[]=foo&tags[]=bar - Common in some ecosystems, but not universal If you choose repeated keys, document it and ensure your server framework parses it correctly (some parse to last-value unless configured). Also decide whether tags filtering means “has any of these tags” (OR) or “has all of these tags” (AND). Many APIs default to OR and offer tags_mode=all if needed.
Good point on OR vs AND. I think default OR is fine. Another question: should I use snake_case or camelCase in JSON?

Either is fine, but consistency matters more than the choice. Consider: - If your clients are mostly JS/TS: camelCase tends to feel native. - If you want to align with many REST APIs and some backend languages: snake_case is common. Pick one and stick to it across: - Request bodies - Response bodies - Error objects - Query parameter names (ideally match the JSON style) If you choose snake_case, then use created_at, author_id, etc. If camelCase, createdAt, authorId. Also decide how you represent IDs: id vs postId. Most APIs use id inside the resource object, and refer to it as post_id in other contexts if needed.
I’ll probably use snake_case. Now about status codes and error formats: should I always return JSON even for errors? And what should the error schema look like?

Yes—return JSON for errors consistently. It makes client handling straightforward. A simple, effective schema: { "error": { "code": "validation_error", "message": "Invalid request", "details": [ {"field": "title", "issue": "required"}, {"field": "visibility", "issue": "must be one of: public, private"} ], "request_id": "..." } } Guidelines: - Use appropriate HTTP status codes: - 400 invalid request (malformed or failed validation) - 401 unauthenticated - 403 forbidden - 404 not found - 409 conflict (e.g., unique constraint) - 422 unprocessable entity (some teams use this for validation; if you do, be consistent) - 429 rate limited - 500+ server errors - Keep error.code stable and machine-readable. - message can be human-readable. - details is optional and structured. Also consider including a request_id header (and echo it in the body) for debugging.
For 404 vs 403, I’ve seen both for “resource exists but you can’t see it.” Which is better?

This is a policy choice: - Return 404 to avoid revealing existence of resources the caller isn’t authorized to know about (“security through non-disclosure”). - Return 403 when you want clients to differentiate “not found” vs “no access,” and you’re okay with revealing existence. Many public APIs choose: - 404 for “not found or not accessible” for sensitive resources - 403 for cases where the resource is known (e.g., user’s own resource) but action is forbidden If you’re adding auth later, you can start with a simple rule: - If authenticated but lacks permission for an action on a known resource: 403 - If the resource is not in the caller’s visible set: 404 Document this because clients will build logic around it.
I want to support partial updates. Would you recommend PUT or PATCH? And how do you handle null fields?

Prefer PATCH for partial updates. PUT is generally “replace the full resource,” which implies clients should send a complete representation; that’s often inconvenient and risky. For PATCH semantics, define how you interpret fields: - Omitted field: no change - Field present with non-null value: update to that value - Field present with null: either clear the field or reject if not nullable You need a clear contract: - If you allow clearing: null means “set to null” (or unset). - If you don’t: null is validation error. Alternatively, you can use JSON Patch (RFC 6902) or JSON Merge Patch (RFC 7396): - Merge Patch (application/merge-patch+json) is simple: null deletes/clears fields. - JSON Patch is more expressive but more complex. Most small APIs do “PATCH with partial JSON object” and define the null behavior explicitly.
I’ll do PATCH with a partial object and allow null to clear optional fields. Next: Should I include “type” in each resource object? Like {type:"post", id:"..."}.

You don’t need it unless you’re doing polymorphic collections or you want a JSON:API-like format. For a straightforward API, keep it simple: { "id": "...", "title": "...", "body": "...", "created_at": "...", ... } If you later return mixed resource types in one array (e.g., an activity feed that includes posts, comments, likes), then including a type discriminator becomes useful: { "type": "post", "id": "...", ... } But don’t add it everywhere “just in case” unless you already foresee mixed-type responses.
I might eventually want an activity feed. Would you design that as /feed or /activities? And would it return mixed objects or normalized references?

Naming: /activities is clearer and more general than /feed. You can still have /feed as an alias, but /activities suggests an activity log. Representation: - Mixed objects (denormalized): - Pros: fewer round trips; clients can render immediately - Cons: heavier payload; repeated data; more complex schema - Normalized references: - Return activity items with references, then clients fetch related resources - Pros: smaller payload; consistent resource schemas - Cons: N+1 requests unless you support includes/expansion A good compromise is to return activity items with a compact embedded “subject” summary plus IDs: { "id": "...", "verb": "post.created", "actor": {"id":"u1","username":"..."}, "object": {"type":"post","id":"p1","title":"..."}, "created_at": "..." } Optionally support ?include=post,actor to embed full objects. If you add include/expand, define it carefully to avoid unbounded nesting.
About include/expand: I was thinking of ?expand=user or ?include=user. What’s the difference and what’s a good design?

The terms are often used interchangeably, but you can define a clear meaning: - include: include related resources in a side-loaded section (like JSON:API’s “included”) - expand: expand inline fields within each object Example expand: GET /posts?expand=author Response: { "data": [ {"id":"p1","author":{"id":"u1","username":"..."}, ...} ] } Example include: GET /posts?include=authors Response: { "data": [ {"id":"p1","author_id":"u1", ...} ], "included": { "authors": [ {"id":"u1","username":"..."} ] } } Inline expand is easier for clients rendering a list. Side-loading can reduce duplication if many posts share the same author. Either is fine; pick one. If you choose expand, keep it constrained: - expand=author,comments_count (simple) - avoid expand=comments.author.profile with deep nesting unless you really need it
I like inline expand. If I do expand=author, should the author field always be present but null when not expanded? Or should it be absent?

Prefer one of these consistent rules: Option A (presence with minimal data): - Always include author_id. - Include author object only when expanded. - When not expanded: omit author field entirely. Option B (always present but null): - author is null unless expanded. I’d pick Option A. Omitted fields communicate “not requested,” whereas null can be ambiguous (“unknown” vs “no author”). Also, omitting reduces payload size and makes it obvious expansion occurred. Document it: “author is only present when expanded; otherwise use author_id.”
Makes sense. Now, naming: should it be /users or /people? And should IDs be numeric or UUID?

/users is the most conventional if the resource represents accounts. /people can be broader (profiles, contacts), but if you mean “users of the system,” /users is clear. IDs: - Numeric IDs: - shorter, readable - can leak counts/sequencing if that matters - easy indexing - UUIDs/ULIDs: - harder to guess/enumerate - safer to expose publicly - slightly larger, but usually fine - ULID has nice lexicographic ordering If you expect public exposure and want to avoid easy enumeration, use UUID/ULID. If simplicity matters and exposure is limited, numeric can be fine. Many modern APIs choose UUIDs. Also decide whether IDs are opaque strings. Even numeric IDs can be represented as strings in JSON to avoid integer precision issues in JS for very large numbers (though typical IDs won’t hit that). If you use UUIDs, strings are natural.
I’ll use ULIDs as strings. For timestamps: ISO 8601 with Z? Or unix epoch?

ISO 8601 in UTC (with Z) is generally best for APIs: - Human-readable - Interoperable - Works well in logs and debugging Example: "2026-04-02T12:34:56Z" Be consistent: always UTC, always include timezone (Z), and ideally include seconds (and milliseconds if needed). If you include fractional seconds, keep a consistent precision. Unix epoch is compact but less readable and can cause confusion about seconds vs milliseconds. If you do epoch, be explicit (e.g., created_at_ms).
Great. I’m also thinking about idempotency for POST requests, especially for creating posts. Should I support Idempotency-Key?

Supporting Idempotency-Key is a solid idea, especially if clients might retry due to timeouts. Design: - Clients send: Idempotency-Key: <unique token> - Server stores the result keyed by (client identity, idempotency key) for some TTL. - If the same key is reused with the same request semantics, return the same response (status + body). - If reused with different payload, return 409 Conflict (or 422) indicating mismatch. Key details: - Scope the key per endpoint and per authenticated principal (or another client identifier), so different clients can use the same key safely. - TTL: depends on your retry window; 24 hours is common. - Document which endpoints support it (usually POST /posts, POST /comments, etc.). Also consider idempotency for PATCH and DELETE: they’re typically idempotent by nature if designed right.
For rate limiting: do you recommend 429 with headers like X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset?

Yes. 429 Too Many Requests plus rate limit headers is a widely used pattern. Headers: - X-RateLimit-Limit: max requests per window - X-RateLimit-Remaining: remaining in current window - X-RateLimit-Reset: epoch seconds when window resets Also include Retry-After header (seconds or HTTP date). Retry-After is standard and many clients understand it. If you have multiple limits (per-minute, per-day), you can either: - Provide multiple header sets with prefixes (messy), or - Use the standardized RateLimit headers (RFC 9331): RateLimit-Limit, RateLimit-Remaining, RateLimit-Reset. Support is growing. You can include both (standard + legacy X-) if you want compatibility.
I’ll probably do the standard RateLimit headers. Now, about authentication: when I add it, should it be Bearer tokens? API keys? Sessions?

For APIs, Bearer tokens (e.g., JWT or opaque tokens) are common and straightforward: - Authorization: Bearer <token> API keys are simpler but typically less secure if used as the only auth mechanism (often long-lived, no user context, harder rotation). Sessions (cookies) are more typical for browser-based apps. If you foresee third-party clients: - Use OAuth2-style flows (even if simplified) and bearer access tokens. If you want simplicity: - Start with opaque bearer tokens stored server-side. - Add refresh tokens later if needed. JWTs can be fine, but they complicate revocation and require careful signing/rotation. Opaque tokens with server-side lookup are easier to invalidate.
I might do opaque tokens. For endpoints, would you do /auth/login and /auth/logout? Or /sessions?

/sessions is more resource-oriented: - POST /sessions creates a session (login) - DELETE /sessions/{id} deletes a session (logout specific session) - GET /sessions (list active sessions) if you want If you prefer a simpler “auth” namespace: - POST /auth/login - POST /auth/logout (or DELETE /auth/token) But /sessions tends to map nicely to REST semantics. If tokens are the session, then: - POST /tokens - DELETE /tokens/{token_id} You can also do: - POST /oauth/token if you mimic OAuth, but that’s more specialized. Pick one consistent style. /sessions is easy to explain.
Nice. Switching gears: Should I use plural nouns everywhere? Like /posts, /comments, /tags. Any exceptions?

Plural nouns for collections is the common convention: - GET /posts (collection) - POST /posts - GET /posts/{id} Exceptions are usually: - Singleton resources: /me, /status, /health - Actions that aren’t resources (but try to avoid): /posts/{id}/publish For singleton: /me is handy for “current user”: - GET /me - PATCH /me For health checks: /health or /status. Otherwise, keep plural for consistency.
About actions like publish: I want posts to have states (draft, published). How do you model “publish” and “unpublish”?

Prefer modeling state transitions as updates to a resource rather than RPC-style actions. Option A: PATCH /posts/{id} with {"status":"published"} - Simple - Auditable if you store status changes Option B: Dedicated subresource: - POST /posts/{id}/publishes (create a publish event) - DELETE /posts/{id}/publishes/{publish_id} (if you want) This is more complex but useful if publishing is an entity with metadata (scheduled time, channel). Most cases: PATCH with status is enough. Also consider: - published_at timestamp set by server when status becomes published - validation: cannot publish without required fields - idempotency: publishing an already published post should be 200 with unchanged status (or 409 if you want stricter semantics; I’d do idempotent 200).
I’ll go with PATCH status. For sorting: should I do sort=created_at&order=desc or sort=-created_at?

Both are common. sort=-created_at is compact and scales to multiple fields: - sort=-created_at,title (minus indicates desc) But it’s a bit less explicit. sort=created_at&order=desc is explicit but gets verbose for multiple sort keys. A good scalable pattern: - sort=-created_at,id Where fields are comma-separated, and a leading - means descending. Document allowed sort fields and default sort. Also guarantee deterministic ordering: include id as a tiebreaker.
For search q, do you recommend simple substring matching or full-text semantics? I’m mostly concerned with the API shape, not implementation.

API-wise, keep it simple: - q=... as a free-text query - Optional fields to scope search: search_in=title,body or just define default scope - Document that q is interpreted as “full-text search” semantics (tokenization) if you plan that, but you can start with substring. If you later add advanced search operators, be careful: clients may start relying on quirks. Consider keeping q as “best effort” and add a structured search endpoint if you need strict semantics. Also decide how q interacts with other filters: typically AND (q plus tag filter narrows results). That’s what clients expect.
Good. Now, response envelope: Should I wrap everything in {data: ...} or return raw arrays/objects?

Wrapping in an envelope ({data: ...}) has benefits: - You can add meta, links, errors consistently without changing the top-level type. - For list endpoints, you can include pagination info. Returning raw objects is simpler but makes it harder to add metadata later without breaking clients. A common compromise: - Always envelope list responses: { "data": [...], "links": {...}, "meta": {...} } - For single resources, you can either: - also envelope: { "data": {...} } - or return raw object If you want maximum consistency and future-proofing, envelope everything. If you want minimal verbosity, envelope only lists. I’d lean toward enveloping everything if you’re designing from scratch.
I’ll envelope everything for consistency. For deletes: should DELETE /posts/{id} return 204 No Content or 200 with a body?

Prefer 204 No Content for successful deletes. It’s clean and conventional. Return 200 with a body if you want to return something useful (e.g., the deleted resource representation, or a tombstone), but many clients don’t need it. Also define behavior if the resource doesn’t exist: - 404 Not Found (common) - Or 204 (idempotent delete semantics). Many APIs still return 404 because it helps catch client bugs. Either is fine; document it.
I’ll use 204 and 404 for missing. Now, about tags: should tags be a separate resource (/tags) or just strings on posts?

Depends on whether tags have their own properties. If tags are just labels: - Keep them as strings on posts: "tags": ["api","design"] - Support filtering: GET /posts?tag=api If tags have metadata (description, color, counts, aliases): - Make tags a resource: - GET /tags - GET /tags/{tag} - Maybe POST /tags for creation if tags aren’t free-form Even if tags are free-form strings, you can still provide a convenience endpoint: - GET /tags?prefix=ap - returns popular tags, counts But don’t force tags into a resource model unless you need it.
For now tags are strings, but I want an endpoint to suggest tags. Maybe GET /tags?query=ap. Is that okay even if tags aren’t a full resource?

Yes. /tags can still be a collection resource even if each tag is essentially identified by its name. You can represent each tag as: { "name": "api", "count": 123 } Endpoints: - GET /tags?query=ap (suggest) - GET /tags?limit=20 (top tags) - GET /tags/{name} (details, optional) Even if you don’t allow creating tags explicitly, listing and fetching them is still resource-like. Be careful with URL encoding for tag names in /tags/{name}. If tags can contain spaces or slashes, it gets tricky. You can restrict tag charset (recommended) or use an ID.
I’ll restrict tags to [a-z0-9-_]. Good. Next, content negotiation: Should I support gzip? And do I need ETags?

gzip (or brotli) is usually handled at the HTTP server/proxy layer, not in your application logic. You can support it by enabling compression and honoring Accept-Encoding. It’s worth it for JSON payloads. ETags: - Useful for caching and conditional requests: - GET returns ETag - Client can send If-None-Match to get 304 Not Modified - Also useful for optimistic concurrency: - Client sends If-Match on PATCH/PUT to avoid lost updates If you want robust concurrency control, ETags are a good tool. Alternatively, you can use a version field (e.g., updated_at timestamp or integer version) and require it on updates. For a small API: - Implement ETags for GET /posts/{id} and maybe list endpoints later. - Implement If-Match for PATCH /posts/{id} to prevent overwriting changes.
Optimistic concurrency sounds useful. If I use updated_at, can that be the ETag?

You can base ETag on updated_at, but be careful: - updated_at may not change for certain updates if you forget to bump it. - resolution: if updated_at is seconds-only, two updates in the same second could collide. Better: - Use a monotonically incrementing version integer, or - Use a hash of the canonical representation, or - Use updated_at with high precision plus id. Simplest robust approach: - Maintain a version integer that increments on each update. - ETag: W/"<version>" (weak ETag) or "<version>" (strong) depending on semantics. - Return version in the resource too. Then: - Clients send If-Match: "<version>" on PATCH. - If mismatch: 412 Precondition Failed.
I like version integers. I’ll do that. Now, I’m worried about naming consistency: user_id vs author_id. Should posts have author_id or user_id?

Choose the domain term that matches the relationship. If a post’s creator is a user, both are true, but “author” is more expressive and avoids confusion when there are multiple user relationships (e.g., editor_id, last_modified_by_id). So: - posts: author_id - expand=author returns author object - /users/{id}/posts is still okay as “posts by user,” but the field is author_id. Similarly for comments: - commenter_id or author_id (often author_id across content types is consistent). Consistency across content types helps.
Great. For comments, I’m deciding between /posts/{id}/comments and /comments?post_id=... Similar to the earlier question. Same recommendation?

Yes, same reasoning. Comments are strongly associated with posts, so nested reads are very common: - GET /posts/{id}/comments is very readable. But if you want uniform filtering (by author, since, moderation status), top-level works: - GET /comments?post_id=...&author_id=...&since=... You can do both, but keep canonical. If comments will also exist independently (e.g., user comment history), top-level is natural. A common setup: - GET /posts/{id}/comments (convenience) - GET /comments?post_id=... (canonical) - POST /posts/{id}/comments (creation convenience) - GET/PATCH/DELETE /comments/{commentId} (single resource)
For creating comments, I like POST /posts/{id}/comments. If the server infers author from auth, that’s clean. Should the response include the full comment object?

Yes—return 201 Created with the created comment representation and a Location header pointing to /comments/{id}. Response: - Status: 201 - Headers: Location: /v1/comments/{id} - Body: { "data": { ...comment... } } This gives the client immediate access to server-generated fields (id, timestamps, version).
Now, what about bulk operations? Like deleting multiple posts or updating tags on many posts. Should I do /posts/bulk?

Bulk operations are where “pure REST” can get awkward, so pragmatic endpoints are common. Options: 1) POST /posts/bulk-delete with body {ids:[...]} 2) POST /posts/bulk with an operation field: { "op":"delete", "ids":[...] } 3) Use a batch endpoint: POST /batch with an array of subrequests (more complex) If you need only a couple bulk operations, explicit endpoints are fine: - POST /posts/bulk_delete - POST /posts/bulk_tag Try to keep them: - Clearly named - Explicit about atomicity: is it all-or-nothing or best-effort per item? - Return per-item results: { "data": { "results": [ {"id":"...","status":"deleted"}, {"id":"...","error": {...}} ] } } Also consider rate limiting and authorization checks per item.
I’ll probably avoid bulk for now. Another design question: should I expose internal database IDs or use separate public IDs?

Prefer a single opaque public ID that you use everywhere. If your internal IDs are safe and stable, you can use them directly. But many designs separate: - internal numeric primary key - external ULID/UUID public_id Since you’re already leaning ULID, you can use that as the primary identifier in the API. Internally you can still store a numeric key if you want, but clients never see it. Key: IDs should be stable and never recycled.
Good. I also want to document the API. OpenAPI/Swagger?

Yes—OpenAPI is the standard choice. Benefits: - Generates interactive docs - Enables client SDK generation - Helps validate requests/responses Tips: - Define schemas for all resources and error responses. - Use components for reuse: Post, Comment, User, Error, PaginationLinks. - Document query params and allowed values. - Add examples; they’re surprisingly helpful. Also consider describing authentication in OpenAPI (securitySchemes) even if you stub it initially.
For OpenAPI, if I envelope everything in {data: ...}, should I define separate schemas like PostResponse and PostsResponse?

Yes. Define: - Post (the resource) - PostResponse: { data: Post } - PostsResponse: { data: [Post], links: ..., meta: ... } Similarly for errors: - ErrorResponse: { error: ErrorObject } This keeps resource schemas reusable. Also consider generic wrappers, but OpenAPI generics are limited. You can still keep it manageable by naming patterns consistently.
Another thought: should I allow clients to select fields, like ?fields=title,created_at to reduce payload?

Field selection can be useful, but it adds complexity: - You must ensure authorization rules still apply (don’t leak hidden fields). - You must handle nested fields and expansions. If you do it, define a clear syntax: - fields=title,created_at,author_id - For expanded objects: fields[posts]=title,created_at&fields[users]=username (more complex) For a small API, you can skip it initially. Compression + reasonable defaults often suffice. If payload size becomes an issue, add it later in a backward-compatible way.
I’ll skip for now. For boolean query params like include_deleted, should it be include_deleted=true/false or 1/0?

Use true/false strings. They’re clearer and common. Accepting 1/0 in addition can be a convenience, but document canonical values. Also decide default behavior: - include_deleted defaults to false - Only allow true for authorized callers (if you have soft deletes)
Soft deletes: I might want to implement them. How should the API reflect that? Add deleted_at field and hide deleted by default?

That’s a common approach: - Resource includes deleted_at (nullable) and maybe is_deleted boolean (redundant but convenient). - By default, list and get endpoints exclude soft-deleted resources. - Add include_deleted=true for privileged access or for the owner. Delete semantics: - DELETE /posts/{id} performs soft delete. - Optional: POST /posts/{id}/restore or PATCH status to restore (or PATCH deleted_at=null if you allow that). Be careful: if you hide deleted resources, DELETE on an already-deleted resource could return 404, which might be fine. Or you can make delete idempotent and return 204 even if already deleted (but then you need to decide whether you allow re-delete).
I’ll keep it simple and return 404 for deleted resources unless include_deleted=true. Now, for content types: should I always use application/json? Any reason to support other formats?

application/json is enough for most APIs. Unless you have a specific need (CSV exports, file downloads), keep it JSON-only. If you add exports later: - GET /posts.csv or Accept: text/csv could work, but it complicates caching and docs. - Better: dedicated export endpoint that returns a file with correct Content-Type. For uploads (images), you might use multipart/form-data. That’s a separate design area.
I do want image uploads for posts later. How would you model that? /uploads?

A common design is to separate media from posts: - POST /uploads (multipart/form-data) → returns an upload resource with URL, id, metadata - Then in post creation/update, reference upload IDs or URLs. Alternatively, direct-to-object-storage flows: - POST /uploads returns a pre-signed URL and fields for direct upload. - Client uploads to storage, then confirms: - POST /uploads/complete with token, or - POST /media with metadata If you want simple: - POST /media with multipart file Response: { "data": { "id":"m1", "url":"https://...", "content_type":"image/png", "size":12345 } } Then posts can have media_ids: ["m1"] or embedded media objects.
I’ll likely do /media. For linking, should I store absolute URLs or relative paths?

In responses, absolute URLs are convenient for clients, but they can cause issues across environments. Relative URLs are more portable. A good compromise: - Provide absolute URLs for externally accessible media (since clients need them). - For API links (pagination, resource links), you can use relative paths if you document base URL. You can also include both: - url (absolute) - path (relative) But that’s extra. For media, absolute is often best.
Okay. Another area: consistent naming for endpoints. Should I use hyphens or underscores in paths? Like /rate-limits vs /rate_limits.

Prefer hyphens in paths if you need multi-word segments; they’re more common in URLs. But many APIs use underscores too. The key is consistency. Examples: - /rate-limits - /access-tokens - /password-resets If you already use snake_case in JSON, that doesn’t force underscores in paths. You can mix (snake_case JSON, hyphen paths) without issue.
I’ll use hyphens in paths. For query params, I’ll keep snake_case. Is that okay?
Yes, that’s a common combo: - Paths: kebab-case - Query params + JSON fields: snake_case Just be consistent and document it.
I want to add a “like” feature. Would that be POST /posts/{id}/likes and DELETE /posts/{id}/likes/{like_id}? Or something else?

Likes are a relationship between a user and a post. If a like is essentially “current user likes this post,” you can model it as a singleton relationship: Option A (current user like): - PUT /posts/{id}/like (idempotent “ensure liked”) - DELETE /posts/{id}/like (ensure unliked) - GET /posts/{id}/like (optional, returns whether liked) Option B (likes collection): - POST /posts/{id}/likes (creates like) - DELETE /likes/{likeId} (or /posts/{id}/likes/{likeId}) Option A is very client-friendly and avoids needing like IDs. It assumes auth and that each user can like at most once. You can still expose: - GET /posts/{id}/likes?limit=... (list users who liked) But for the actor’s action, PUT/DELETE on a singleton is elegant. If you don’t have auth yet, you might need like IDs, but once auth exists, I’d prefer the singleton approach.
I like the singleton approach. So PUT /posts/{id}/like returns 204?

Either 204 No Content or 200 with a representation. For idempotent relationship toggles: - PUT returns 204 - DELETE returns 204 If you want to return updated like_count, you can return 200 with: { "data": { "liked": true, "like_count": 42 } } But that’s optional. You can also rely on clients refreshing the post or using websockets later. Keep it simple: 204 is fine.
How do you recommend representing counts like like_count and comment_count? Store them on the post object?

It’s common to include denormalized counts on the post object: - like_count - comment_count They’re useful for list views. Just document that: - They may be eventually consistent (if your system is distributed). - They represent visible items (e.g., exclude deleted comments). If you include them, ensure updates are correct and that they don’t become a performance bottleneck. But API-wise, it’s very convenient.
For comment_count, should it include replies? I might support threaded comments.

Define it explicitly: - comment_count: total number of comments (including replies) visible - Or top_level_comment_count: number of top-level comments If you support threads, clients often need both. You can include: - comment_count (total) - top_level_comment_count And for comment resources, include: - parent_id (nullable) - depth (optional) - reply_count (optional) For listing comments: - GET /posts/{id}/comments?parent_id=null (top-level) - GET /comments?parent_id=... (replies) Or: - GET /posts/{id}/comments?threaded=true (returns nested structure) — but nested can be heavy.
I think I’ll do parent_id and list top-level by default. Threaded rendering can fetch replies lazily. Great.

That’s a scalable approach. Default to top-level keeps payload small and avoids deep recursion. Provide: - GET /posts/{id}/comments?parent_id=<commentId> - Or /comments?parent_id=<commentId> Also consider sorting: top-level by created_at desc, replies by created_at asc, etc. Document defaults.
Another design issue: should I allow clients to request “my posts” without specifying user_id? Like GET /posts?author=me or GET /me/posts.

Both patterns exist. - GET /me/posts is straightforward and avoids special query syntax. - GET /posts?author=me is compact but introduces a special token value. I’d recommend: - GET /me/posts for “current user’s posts” - Still allow GET /posts?author_id=... for general filtering Similarly for likes: - GET /me/likes or /me/liked-posts Singleton /me is a nice convenience that reduces clients needing to know their user ID.
I’ll add /me and /me/posts. For /me, should it be a redirect to /users/{id}?

You can implement it as: - GET /me returns the same schema as GET /users/{id} No need to redirect; redirects can complicate clients and aren’t always handled uniformly. But you can include a link: - "links": {"self": "/v1/me", "user": "/v1/users/{id}"} And ensure that PATCH /me behaves like PATCH /users/{id} for the authenticated user.
Good. For link relations, do you recommend a standard structure? Just a "links" object with URLs?

A simple "links" object is fine: - links.self - links.next - links.prev - links.related (optional) If you want more standardization, you can use HAL-like: "_links": { "self": {"href": ...}, "next": {"href": ...} } But that’s more verbose. Given you already have envelopes, a plain links map is pragmatic. Just keep URLs consistent and consider whether they are relative or absolute.
Great. Now, I’m thinking about HTTP methods for updating collections. For example, setting tags list on a post: should that be PATCH /posts/{id} with tags array, or PUT /posts/{id}/tags?

If tags are just a field of the post, PATCH /posts/{id} with "tags": [...] is simplest. If tags are a subresource with its own semantics (like membership management), /posts/{id}/tags can be nice: - PUT /posts/{id}/tags replaces the full tag list - PATCH /posts/{id}/tags modifies (add/remove) with a specific schema But that adds another endpoint surface. Unless you need separate permissions or specialized operations, keep it in the post PATCH.
Understood. How would you handle validation errors for tags? Return which tag is invalid?

Yes. In error.details include index and value: { "error": { "code": "validation_error", "message": "Invalid tags", "details": [ {"field":"tags[2]","issue":"invalid_format","value":"Bad Tag"} ] } } Also validate: - max number of tags - max length - allowed chars - uniqueness (no duplicates) Return 400 or 422 depending on your convention.
I’ll use 422 for validation failures. Is that okay?

Yes. Many APIs use: - 400 for malformed JSON / invalid types / missing required top-level structure - 422 for semantically invalid fields Just be consistent and document it. Also ensure your clients treat 4xx broadly as client errors.
Another topic: Should I use HTTP caching for list endpoints? Like Cache-Control?

If content is public and changes infrequently, caching can help. But for user-specific feeds or authenticated content, caching is trickier. Basic approach: - For public GET endpoints: set Cache-Control: public, max-age=... - For authenticated endpoints: Cache-Control: private, maybe no-store for sensitive data - Use ETag for conditional GET to reduce bandwidth. Even if you don’t tune caching initially, adding ETags later is easier if your API is consistent.
I’ll start without aggressive caching, but I’ll include ETag on single-resource GET.
That’s a good incremental plan.
Now, I want to ensure my API is consistent about “empty list” responses. Should it be data: [] and links/meta present, or can I omit links?

Prefer consistency: - data: [] - links: { self: ..., next: null, prev: null } - meta: { limit: ..., ... } (optional but consistent) Even if next/prev are null, including them avoids clients needing special cases.
Great. For “not found,” should the error body be the same schema with error.code="not_found"?
Yes. For 404: { "error": { "code": "not_found", "message": "Post not found", "request_id": "..." } } Avoid leaking internal details. Keep the code stable.
Another question: Should I support HEAD requests for resources?

It’s optional. HEAD can be useful for: - Checking existence without fetching body - Getting headers like ETag, Last-Modified But many APIs don’t implement it explicitly; frameworks may handle it automatically for GET. If you implement it, ensure it returns the same headers as GET but no body. Not critical unless you have a use case.
Probably skip. Now, I’m designing an endpoint for “recent posts” vs “all posts.” Should that be /posts/recent or just /posts with default sort?

Prefer /posts with sort/pagination. “Recent” is usually just: - GET /posts?sort=-created_at If you want a curated or special algorithmic feed, then a separate endpoint makes sense: - GET /posts/recommended - GET /posts/trending Because those aren’t simple sorts; they’re different semantics. For “recent,” keep it as sorting.
Good. For trending, would you do /posts/trending? What query params would it accept?

/posts/trending is fine if it’s a distinct collection. Params might include: - window=24h / 7d - limit - cursor - tag (optional) - visibility (if applicable) Define defaults: - window=24h - sort by a trending score desc Return same Post representation. Also keep consistent pagination style. If trending is computed, cursor may need to be based on score+id.
Nice. Now, I’m thinking about how to represent visibility: public/private/unlisted. Should it be a string enum field?

Yes: - visibility: "public" | "private" | "unlisted" Document semantics: - public: visible in public listings and accessible by anyone (if no auth) - unlisted: accessible by direct link but not listed - private: only visible to owner (and maybe collaborators) If unlisted requires a secret slug, you might add: - share_token or slug But be careful with security: a guessable token defeats unlisted.
I might add a share_token later. For now, unlisted is just “not in list but accessible by ID,” which isn’t truly unlisted. I’ll revisit.
That’s fine as long as you document it. “Unlisted” without a secret is more like “not featured” than unlisted, but you can still keep the enum and refine later.
Now, for resource identifiers in URLs: should I allow clients to fetch by slug, like /posts/{slug}? Or keep slugs separate?

If you want both ID and slug, avoid ambiguity. Options: - Keep /posts/{id} for IDs only, and add /posts/by-slug/{slug} - Or use a query: /posts?slug=... - Or use a separate field in path with prefix: /posts/slug/{slug} If you overload /posts/{identifier} to accept both, you risk collisions and confusion. A clean design: - GET /posts/{id} - GET /posts/by-slug/{slug} And in post representation include: - id - slug So clients can choose.
Great. Another thing: consistent content-type for requests. For PATCH and POST, should I require Content-Type: application/json and reject otherwise?

Yes. Require Content-Type: application/json for JSON bodies and return 415 Unsupported Media Type if missing/incorrect. Also handle: - Invalid JSON: 400 with a parse error code - Wrong type: 400 or 422 depending on your scheme (I’d do 400 for type mismatch at top level, 422 for validation) This makes behavior predictable.
For parse errors, should I return details like “unexpected token at position…”?

You can, but be careful not to leak internal parser details. A safe approach: - message: "Malformed JSON" - details: maybe line/column if available, but not raw stack traces Example: { "error": { "code": "invalid_json", "message": "Malformed JSON in request body" } } If you can provide a pointer, that’s helpful, but not required.
Now, I want to design a “report post” endpoint for moderation. Would that be POST /posts/{id}/reports?

Yes, that’s a good model: reports as a subresource collection under posts: - POST /posts/{id}/reports Body: { "reason": "spam", "details": "..." } Then moderators can list reports: - GET /reports?status=open or - GET /posts/{id}/reports If you expect reports to apply to multiple object types (posts, comments), you can generalize: - POST /reports with {"object_type":"post","object_id":"...","reason":"..."} But per-resource nested endpoints are more readable. Also consider idempotency: you might prevent duplicate reports by same user for same post (return 409 or return existing report).
I’ll do nested for now. For moderation status: should posts have a “moderation_state” field? Or should it be separate?

If moderation affects visibility/access, having moderation_state on the post is convenient: - moderation_state: "clean" | "flagged" | "removed" | "under_review" But be careful: exposing it to all clients may leak internal moderation info. You can: - Include moderation_state only for authorized callers (expand=moderation or role-based fields). - Or keep it separate as a moderation resource. If you don’t have roles yet, keep moderation separate to avoid committing to exposure. Alternatively, include a simple "status" that clients can understand (active/removed) and keep internal states hidden.
I’ll keep it separate for now. Another design: Should the API return consistent ordering of keys in JSON?

Clients shouldn’t rely on key ordering in JSON; it’s not significant. You can keep consistent ordering for readability in docs/examples, but don’t guarantee it. If you generate ETags based on JSON, don’t hash raw serialized JSON unless you canonicalize it, because key order can change. Better to base ETags on version numbers as you planned.
Great. For testing the API, I’m writing a client in TypeScript. Should I generate types from OpenAPI or handwrite them?

Generating from OpenAPI is usually worth it: - Keeps client in sync - Reduces manual errors - Encourages you to keep spec accurate You can still add hand-written wrappers for ergonomics. If you generate, consider: - Use a tool that supports your fetch/axios preference - Ensure it handles nullable fields and optional fields correctly - Decide whether to generate runtime validators (zod) or just types A hybrid approach: generate types + minimal client, then build a nicer SDK on top.
That’s the plan. Now, I’m worried about backwards compatibility. If I add a new field to Post, will it break clients?

It shouldn’t, as long as clients are tolerant: - JSON consumers should ignore unknown fields - Avoid changing meaning of existing fields - Avoid changing types (string → object) without version bump - Avoid removing fields or making required fields optional/required unexpectedly Document that responses may include additional fields. In your own client, ensure you don’t do exact object matching. Use pick/partial parsing.
For enum expansions (adding a new visibility value), could that break clients?

Yes, if clients treat enums as exhaustive. Best practice: - Clients should handle unknown enum values gracefully (e.g., display “unknown”). - In TypeScript, this is tricky because unions are often exhaustive. You can include a fallback string type or handle default case. API-wise, adding enum values is a backward-compatible change in theory, but in practice it can break strict clients. If you foresee adding values, consider: - Using strings and documenting possible values, but not guaranteeing they’re exhaustive - Or adding a separate “capabilities” endpoint so clients know supported values But many APIs still evolve enums; just be cautious.
Capabilities endpoint sounds interesting. What would it look like?

Something like: - GET /capabilities Response: { "data": { "visibility_values": ["public","private","unlisted"], "post_sort_fields": ["created_at","updated_at"], "max_page_limit": 100, "features": { "idempotency_key": true, "expand_author": true } } } It helps clients adapt dynamically, but it’s optional. If you control clients, you may not need it. It can be useful for UI configuration.
I’ll probably skip it. Another: consistent formatting for IDs in error messages. Should I echo invalid IDs?

You can include the invalid ID in error.details for debugging, but avoid echoing sensitive tokens. For IDs (ULIDs), it’s fine. Example: { "error": { "code": "not_found", "message": "Post not found", "details": [{"id":"01H..."}] } } But keep message generic. Also consider that echoing user input can be a vector for log injection or confusion, though with ULIDs it’s low risk.
Okay. I think I’ve got a good handle on design. Could you help me draft a small set of endpoints and example request/response shapes for posts and comments, including pagination and expand?

Sure. Here’s a cohesive minimal set (v1, envelope everywhere, cursor pagination, expand=author). "Invalid request", "details": [ {"field":"title","issue":"required"} ], "request_id": "req_..." } } If you want, I can also propose a minimal OpenAPI component layout for these.

This is great. Can we talk about one more thing: how should I handle “embedded resources” like author when expanded, to avoid circular references? Like author contains posts, posts contain author, etc.

Avoid embedding recursively. Define “summary” representations for expanded related objects. For example: - UserSummary: { id, username, created_at } - User: full user resource (maybe includes bio, settings, etc.) When you expand author inside Post, use UserSummary only. Don’t include the author’s posts inside that summary. Similarly, if you expand comments’ author, use UserSummary. You can enforce this with your schema: - Post.author: UserSummary - Comment.author: UserSummary If a client needs full author details, they can GET /users/{id} separately or you can provide expand=author_full if you really want, but that tends to bloat payloads. Also be careful with expand on list endpoints: expanding author for 100 posts can duplicate the same author data. If that’s a concern, consider side-loading includes later.
That makes sense. I think I’m done. Thanks for all the help.

Glad to help. If you later want to sanity-check edge cases (idempotency + retries, cursor encoding, or OpenAPI examples), share the proposed spec shape and I can review it for consistency and pitfalls.
